Don't believe me? Gosudar-batyushka, who even has the time to bother with this rabble? If we killed them, then what, are we supposed to go into the mines ourselves to dig?
Eek—! What are you doing?! Okay, I'll shut up, I'll shut up now!
This isn't just happening in one or two mines... All the Far North, the entire mining district, all of us in it... We've been abandoned.
We heard Rankin was the first one to step up. He got rid of all the soldiers, and led several hundred people on an escape.
And now we've escaped too. We wanted to find his group and leave with them, but they're far away by now.
Okay, we've told you enough. Since you're Infected too, you should get out of the way and let us kill these bastards!
Sorry, but if I may be brutally blunt for a moment.
I can overlook the reasoning of why you have left your mine, and treat you as asylum-seekers for the time being.
But if you actually do kill these unfortunate souls, then things will not go well for you.
(Eloysius! You can't speak to them like that!)
What's with the officer in the pretty clothes? Blyat! I never should have trusted you people!
The appearance of an Imperial Guardswoman relights the flames of the miners' ire. They lift up their weapons once more to fence you in, but in fear of your Originium Arts, they dare not act rashly.
As for the routed Patrol Unit, the moment they see Eloysius, it is as if they have met their savior. They huddle behind her, cowering.
It is altogether a rather awkward, irreconcilable scene.
It looks like if we don't resolve this situation, we cannot continue onwards, can we?

opts: Things being what they are, we have no choice.

lines.x: Very well...
The Guardswoman pulls out some paper from her coat pocket, quickly writing down a few words on it before sealing it within an ornate envelope and handing it to the head miner.
Listen up. I have a suggestion that will make everyone happy.
Take this letter. The seal on the envelope will ensure that no Patrol Units harass you, so long as you do not provoke them.
Head north, to Bresk's sentry post. Hand the letter to the soldiers there, and they will find temporary shelter for you. As for what comes next... discuss it only after getting there.
Believe me when I say this is the only way to a peaceful resolution. In exchange, can you find it in your hearts to let these poor whelps go?
N-No! You can't!
If we don't bring them back, we'll be disciplined!
Enough. You're lucky to be alive as it is.
Why should we trust you?
Do you have anyone else you can trust?
Eloysius!
We...
If we go to this shelter you mentioned, will there be enough bread for everyone?
Rest assured. If bread is what you need, then bread you shall have.
The head miner takes the proffered letter, then turns to discuss the offer in hushed tones with the other miners.
They quickly come to a consensus, and with no hesitation whatsoever, the miners form one long line and start heading north, as if entering the mine, just like any other day.
The long stretch of miners slowly funnels through a gap between the huts. All the while, Amiya keeps looking towards the north, the procession fading into the distance.

opts: This is the best outcome we could hope for under the circumstances.

lines.x: I understand, Doctor.
We can't save... can't help everyone we meet.
But Rhodes Island will keep pressing on... until the last of our strength has left us.
You turn to look at the Imperial Guardswoman behind you, seemingly frivolous yet always able to take the initiative.

opts: We never heard of any supply shortage in the mining district.
This is no ordinary control measure.

lines.x: Yes, I know what you are about to ask me. Believe me, I know about as much as you do. We are of like mind when it comes to these misgivings.
The research institute is not far up ahead. Let us go and find answers there.
Let's go, Doctor.

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District Laboratory, Ursus

lines.x: As you walk along the corridor, the echoes of your footsteps are swallowed up by its unending length.
Its stone walls lean inwards, as if their grave majesty were about to smother everything upon the ground. Those who walk along it find themselves lowering their voices, then lowering them further.
(Whisper) It's so quiet here...
Rather, it seems there are no Infected here. Or anyone, for that matter.

opts: Which means...?

lines.x: I've been to several Army research institutes before. They tend to be places of chaos, with plenty of people coming and going. Sickbeds that don't fit in the wards are put in the corridors, not too different from a battlefield hospital.
There is yelling, wailing, screaming from the Infected, but at least they live, at least they struggle on. But here...
Hmph. Nothing.

opts: There are traces of fighting in the corridor.
Was the institute looted?

lines.x: The entire district appears to be in terrible disarray.
If all the abnormal Infected from the institute have escaped...
The soldiers of the district have been gravely derelict in their duty. Their leader will not shirk responsibility.
Who knows, there may be great change coming to all of Breskenoy...

opts: If the miners have been here, there must be some clues to find.
We need to find out what happened here.

lines.x: While we have no accurate blueprints on hand, these sorts of buildings usually follow a standard layout.

If things are as expected, this hallway should form a loop, with standardized linked rooms adjoining. Every floor will also have an enclosed observation deck, for easier viewing of what's going on in the mining area.
Are you able to detect any of those 'biosignals' you mentioned earlier?
(Silence)
Sorry, ma'am, but if you were hoping we could trace signals to a specific room in here, then no can do.
The best we can do is confirm signal waves are coming from a certain area. You'll need to go there and inspect things yourself if you want to find the source, like we're doing right now.
Alright, I was simply asking.
This is a big institute, so I'll head down to the basement to have a look. In the interests of efficiency, how about we split up?

opts: It's dangerous to be alone here.
(Look at Amiya)

lines.x: I'll go with you, Eloysius.
Sure, sure. I don't mind having someone else helping out.

opts: Mon3tr and I will check upstairs then.
Mantra, you and your team search the ground floor.
Maintain comms at all times.
Prioritize your own safety, everyone.

lines.x: (Nods)
This is a pretty spooky place, and it's an Ursine research institute. Who knows what strange things are hidden here, like maybe they were experimenting with fusing humans and burdenbeasts...
Mon3tr, if anything goes wrong, get the Doctor out of here pronto, no fighting!
I know, I know!

opts: Mantra.
Report.

lines.x: I am here.
We have found nothing unusual at our current position.
I have eyes on Amiya. She is safe.

opts: About that biosignal.
We can trace it more precisely than we let on, correct?

lines.x: Fourth floor, mineshaft side. Target is not moving.
Wha?
You never told me you could do that, Captain!

opts: We need to be on guard.
Deception is not your strong suit.
I hope she's the Ksenia Mon3tr mentioned.

lines.x: We are short on time, Doctor. The target's vital signs are unstable.
You seemed really sure we would find something down here, Eloysius—
Thanks to a little 'laziness', many Ursine government buildings follow the same standardized layout.
If this institute is like the others I've visited, especially when it comes to how they divide up the space for specific uses, then there should be something left in the basement.
The dead cannot get up and leave, after all.
Do you mean this basement is a morgue?
A temporary one, with incinerators equipped to handle the remains of the severely infected.
Aha.
I knew it.
Although power to the freezing mechanism had been cut, the basement was still colder than the snow-dusted outdoors.
The young Guardswoman runs her hand across the coffins, which leaves behind a layer of frosty condensation on her fingers.
These coffins have been used. They must have lost power just before the evacuation. Could you take the right side of the room, Gospozha Amiya? We'll open them up one by one to check—
Eloysius!
What is it?
Oh, how careless of me. You might never have seen a corpse before... Then call someone over. No need to trouble yourself.
No, that's not important right now!
The Infected remains are very dangerous. If they have disintegrated and we open the coffin lids, this entire room might become a high-Originium-dust environme—
Before Amiya has even finished speaking, the Guardswoman pushes open a coffin.
There is no release of Originium dust, yet Eloysius's expression turns grave.
What is going on here...?
Eloysius does not answer, instead turning to open another coffin, then yet another.
The corpses interred here...
None of them are Infected.
Wait. Stay back, Amiya.
They must have been performing some kind of experiments on the uninfected. That's all I can glean here, and that's all you need to know.
There is no need for you to... personally examine them.
Thank you for your concern, but I... I think I should.
Amiya anticipated something like this. The moment she stepped into the institute, sorrow shrouded her mind.

It chills and clings to her. Suffering is smeared onto every wall in this place. Humanity's frail fingers are too weak to leave any trace on the stone and steel. Not even blood can find purchase here. All that can remain is hate.
Only hate.
Amiya does her utmost to ignore the spectral arms stretched out towards her. In the silence, she listens to the beating of her heart echoing through her chest.
A dozen or so corpses, all in abject misery.
Each of them has been twisted into incomprehensible forms. Their skin has ruptured, exposing their viscera and bones. They barely even look human.
It is as if their frames had been subject to some enigmatic force, intent on warping them both inside and out until they neared complete destruction.
Who knows how long they were subject to this agony? Who knows how they suffered during this torment?
A-Are you alright?
Amiya does not feel fear.
Rage.
She feels only rage.
...Mantra...
The very next instant, a familiar voice answers.
I am here.
What is it, Amiya?
Eloysius and I are in the morgue in the basement. We can confirm that the institute was conducting large-scale human experimentation. Experimental methods, unknown...
The victims we have discovered are all uninfected. No clues on the abnormal Infected at the moment...
Understood. I will inform the Doctor.
Stay safe, and stay in touch.
We can say that our earlier worries are more clearly defined now.
The situation here, or rather, in the Far North, is much worse than our intelligence suggested.

The strong emotion attacking me in the institute, alongside everything we experienced on our way here, like the Patrol Units harassing the Infected, and the contamination created by the Royal Guard...
All this goes to show that the issues here extend far beyond the Infected mutations.

opts: Amiya and Eloysius found something.
The institute really was conducting illegal human experiments.
However, we're still unsure of the cause of the abnormal infections.
No matter who our target is, there must be some information waiting.

lines.x: Hmm... Where is she, anyway?
I've searched all the rooms on the mineshaft side, and nobody was there.

opts: What about the locked rooms?

lines.x: Same thing. The locks are old-fashioned, easy enough to pry open.
But regardless of the status of their locks, most of the rooms had nothing except for things like empty shelves and cabinets or loose graph paper.
Could she have left?

opts: Or maybe we're looking in the wrong place.

lines.x: What do you mean?

opts: Eloysius says every floor has an enclosed observation deck.

lines.x: I never found anything like—
Wait, that can't be. It's regular rooms the entire way around the loop, and we've checked every single one. There's not enough space in this weird building for an observation deck!

opts: Maybe, on the mineshaft side...
There's a hidden door we haven't discovered yet.
A room within a room.

lines.x: The voice comes out of nowhere, hoarse and fragmented. It creeps upwards from the ankles of the listeners up to their napes, coming to an icy standstill in the back of their minds.
It sounds human, but it is hard to verify.

opts: Did you hear that, Mon3tr?
A voice.

lines.x: (Whispers) I did. Up ahead. I remember the room.
The lights here are broken, so stay behind me, Doctor.
Behind the door is hidden another, this one unlocked. Though the hands of humanity once left their heat upon its handle, now, it is as cold as ice.
Mon3tr pushes open the small, light door. Within the darkness, the visitors are greeted by feeble candlelight.
The first sound they hear is not the voice of a human, but the wet, squelching sound of viscous liquid.
Molten wax drips, melting into the disorderly, blackening dark-red stains on the floor and splattering onto scattered sheets of paper.
And on those sheets... Next to some handprints, a list of formulae is scrawled out, tremblingly penned like screeches of fear and anger.
Meaningless, it's all... meaningless.
Great Ursus sits atop all creation, but its vast domain lies barren...
Aside from never-ending disaster.
Hah... Professor Ilia...
I finally see that you were right!
You were right, absolutely right! But where are you? Where have you gone?
I tried my best, tried so hard! But in the end, it didn't even matter!
I wanted to look for you all. I wanted to. But now, all I can do is take everything away. Take it all... away.
Then...
I will find you.
The woman stretches out her hand, bringing a sheet of paper up to the candle's flame.
The fire soon consumes half of it. She watches it burn with an insatiable gaze, completely unaware of the company by her side.
Ksenia!
You're Ksenia, right?
Mon3tr rushes up to crush the burning paper, extinguishing it with her bare hands before it can grow into an inferno.
She is surprised to find that the slumped-over woman is surrounded by a tangle of wires.
Where do these wires lead? If they had not intervened in time, what would the ensuing blaze have led to?

opts: Did she just say 'Ilia'?

lines.x: She did, I heard it too!
Is that you, Ksenia? You've grown, you look different from before...
Why didn't you leave with everyone else?
No apparent response to sound. She may have entered a dissociative state.

opts: Move her to the side, keep calling her name.

lines.x: Ksenia! We—
I will...
Find you...

opts: These papers on the ground...?

lines.x: You gather up all the sheets that were by her feet, flipping through them by candlelight.
They are not what you imagined, not failed formulae or blank sheets of paper. The words written on them are clear, and are in grammatical Ursine.

lines.st: What I am about to write is not meant to be known by any living soul.
For there is no meaning in leaving behind words about me, or the things I love. What I write here is merely my recollection and repentance, for me to take to my grave.
I believed researching the Sarcophagus was a grand undertaking, with clean energy for the Ursine people close at hand. But the ember of that hope was ultimately snuffed out by our own divisions.

I was fortunate enough to escape that unjust massacre due to my ignorance, but my good fortune was itself unjust, for the price I paid was being forced to live the rest of my life in panicked mediocrity.
I have nowhere to go, no home to return to. The talent I was so proud of is useless. My life has amounted to nothing.

lines.x: ...Ksenia.
I'm Mon3tr. Do you remember... Do you remember Kal'tsit? I'm...
Ksenia!

lines.st: It seems that whenever we are given the opportunity to advance, we always find a reason to hesitate.
This interminable failure of a life has made me truly understand those words I read many years ago in a book, words I once scoffed at:
'The reason why Ursus is constantly flooded with sorrow and injustice is because we lag behind other nations in many aspects.'
'No matter how many truths we discover or accomplishments we make, this backwardness will ruthlessly extinguish the fires of our inspiration.'

opts: This appears to be a suicide note.

lines.x: Still no reaction from her. Does it say where the Sarcophagus ended up after it left Chernobog?

opts: No, but it mentions researching some kind of material.
I think it's describing mutations in Ursine Infected.

lines.st: Oka should have been a chance to turn things around, but just like every other opportunity that passed me by, it was fleeting...
Was my research wrong yet again? Or is this inexplicable, miraculous substance from the mines truly nothing out of the ordinary?

It clearly contains no energy, yet it so callously tortures those patients who are already suffering from disease, driving them mad, making them act in unusual ways, even allowing them to accomplish the impossible.

I do not know, but I am unwilling to be the one who inflicts even more pain on them! Fortunately, I need not continue... That is all I have to say. Now, all I can do is make guesses using metaphysics, like some sort of poet:
Oka may simply be a part of Ursus's essence buried deep in the ground. It revels in the suffering of those it abhors, all behind a mighty, harmless countenance.
Enough. Stop heaping your egotistical, unfounded learning upon this man-made calamity, Ksenia.
Once more, you are a despicable survivor. You should have died twenty years ago at the hands of those soldiers, or here, at the hands of angry Infected.
But now, it all comes to an end.

lines.x: Ksenia...

opts: We know for sure that she's Ksenia.
What is 'Oka'?

lines.x: Oka...
Ksenia?
...can kill... the Royal Guard...
It's dangerous.

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District Laboratory, Ursus

lines.x: Did she just say... 'Royal Guard'?

opts: I heard her say...
Kill the Royal Guard, even.

lines.x: So she means... the substance called 'Oka' killed the Royal Guard?
Is that possible? A sort of demonic product even scarier than their Dominion?

opts: An unidentified substance discovered from the mines.
Used by researchers to experiment on both Infected and non-Infected.
Results proved to worsen Oripathy symptoms.

lines.x: Maybe, it's just a special name for a substance? We can't come to a conclusion before we've seen it ourselves.
Ksenia, Ksenia! Look at me, can you remember anything?
I... you're...
Mon3tr thinks this attempt will be as fruitless as the last, but the woman's glazed eyes suddenly pop wide open.
She immediately clutches the clothes before her, pulling Mon3tr close.
You're—the Director? Director Kal'tsit?
Did you come here for me? I want to go back, I want to see the professor—
I want to see Professor Ilia... and Lyuda... ugh...
But they... I don't think they'll want to see me, right? A coward like me who couldn't do anything... Do I still deserve to see them?
Uwa...ahhh—aaaahh!!!
However, the woman does not wait for Mon3tr to respond before releasing her grip, and returns to her prior position on the floor.
No, something's off...
You're not the Director. I never saw her again, just like the others. She wouldn't be here.
I even saw those who came after her, and they... they...
They? Who did you see? Was one of them a short Liberi who's always smiling?
Liberi... yes, I've seen her.
Where did she go?
I don't know...
The group that killed the Royal Guard... She was with those Infected.
They'll die! Everyone will die!
Ugh... I don't know—I don't know...

opts: She needs a sedative.
She's dehydrated.

lines.x: So the information we've been getting—

opts: The miners' escape is related to the Royal Guard.
Mantra mentioned that the contamination caused by the Royal Guard' is spreading south.
So the answer is obvious.

lines.x: But... did the Royal Guard really get killed? By a bunch of miners? How's that possible?

opts: Perhaps we should consider...
What it means if it is indeed real.

lines.x: The Grand Duchess actually locked the mines down to cover up the fact that a Royal Guard died in her domain?
But that's impossible! Be it the Tsar or the Armies, they won't let the matter die so easily. If she was really trying to cover it up, she wouldn't have agreed to let us in from the start.
Does that Imperial Guard know about this too?

opts: That's right...
That is exactly what we need to consider.
Let's contact Mantra and Amiya first.

lines.x: Roger, Doctor.
Likewise, we have made progress here that coheres to your information.
There are traces of fighting, and some medical equipment lying around.
Rhodes Island standard issue.
We'll meet you ASAP.
Understood... Doctor.
We'll be right there.
What is it? Have your companions discovered something?
I'm afraid we can only explain after we regroup.
Eloysius, are there any other leads here we should be aware of?
There are limits to what I know, and I can't draw any more useful conclusions. If needed, it would be acceptable to let your Doctor come here to investigate.
Though, this only makes me even more certain the Army has no moral baseline whatsoever...
Could I ask if you know anything else about what happened in the mines?
Hm? What do you want to know? The experiments conducted here, or something about the mutated Infected?
What's wrong?
Amiya has always resisted using her powers in such situations and in such a manner, but still she tries to probe the Imperial Guard's mind.
Eloysius's head shows only her hazy doubts, and no guilty conscience.
Nothing. Let's go.
Doctor, these papers... and the suicide note. What should we do with them?

opts: This note barely mentions the Sarcophagus.
That Guardswoman gave us information, so we should repay the favor.

lines.x: Is it over here? What a quest...
Oh, I'm sure there's a secret or two buried here, searching this deep—
—Wait, who is this?
The Guardswoman's gaze rests on the pale-faced woman leaning against the wall, eyes shut.
A researcher who didn't evacuate. Her name is Ksenia.

We wanted to ask her if she's seen our friends, since the researchers should have bumped into our mobile medical team, but she's been in a daze and is severely dehydrated, so it's unwise to press her.
And these candles and wires on the ground...?
My guess is, her attempt to take her own life. Doctor, what exactly happened here?

opts: We came just in time to prevent an explosion.
The situation is complex.

lines.x: And what are those papers in your hand?

opts: A suicide note. You should take a look.
(Hand it to her)

lines.x: A researcher who achieved nothing, a mysterious substance called 'Oka'... and human experimentation.
It seems the dead we saw down below were killed by this 'Oka'.
They were really conducting human experiments here?
Damn, I was just making an offhand remark back there—
(Shakes head)
Hmph, I see the Grand Duchess has truly hidden many things.
But what was the point of the experiment? I'm not surprised the researchers resorted to such base methods, but they should have had some sort of goal!
And this researcher? Hasn't said anything?

opts: It seems like some research materials have been destroyed on purpose.
But it's unclear if that was done by the miners or researchers.

lines.x: So, we still don't know how those mutated Infected came about.
Though, we know now at least that the miners who were still alive have escaped, and save for this one researcher, they're the only clue to cracking this case.

opts: The Infected miners left after ransacking the place.
They went south. Our only clue.

lines.x: We should hurry... How long will it take, until this researcher is well enough to field questions?
Hard to tell. She's severely dehydrated, and in a pretty bad state too, so we should prioritize her recovery.
What a pain...
We don't know how long it's been since they left, but we should grab the chance to catch up, since we know where they went.
Though, there's no way we can bring this woman along with us who can't even move on her own.

opts: Mon3tr and I will stay here to look after Ksenia.
The rest of you, go after the miners.

lines.x: Ah... Are you sure you want to stay here by yourselves?
It is your choice, but I still have to remind you.
No one knows what's going on with the mines in the Far North, so no one can guarantee your safety.

opts: We'll keep ourselves safe.
You can trust in Amiya's command.

lines.x: Doctor?

opts: You can do it, Amiya.
Trust your own judgment.

lines.x: I will go with Amiya.
Doctor, I hope your efforts prove fruitful.

opts: Once Ksenia can move again, we'll come find you.
Signal should gradually improve upon leaving the contaminated zone.

lines.x: So it's settled.
Doctor, if the signal doesn't improve, we'll come back here after we find the miners to regroup with you.
(Nods)
Very good then, that is our backup plan. Let us hurry now.

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District, Ursus Three days ago...

lines.x: Over here... hurry!
Gah, what's the rush? This lousy positioning system works only half the time, it might not even be here.

Last time it was a burdenbeast that escaped just to find some grass to chomp on, and this thing only sent a signal to the outpost after it ran more than ten miles away. How do you know if it won't be the same this time?
Technically, no one should be at the south exit, since it's been sealed for a long time—
Huh? How did you get here from over there?
No, you're not even a member of our squad. What are you doing here?
That's my question to ask. This area isn't under your jurisdiction, is it?
It's past the time to care who's covering where. The one who gets the news and catches the runaway Infected first gets the credit.
Now get lost. You're in my way.
According to you, I was here first, so the credit goes to my squad, no?
Whoever finds those gavno first gets the credit.
What are you doing, Tosha? Stop wasting your time with him and continue your search.
Over here! They're all hiding—Hahahahaaahaha!
What did he find to laugh like that?
Go and see.
There's a hole up ahead, and they're just huddled in there, thinking we wouldn't find them—
O bozhe moy, hahahahahahaha... this is too funny.
Heh. Did they think it was a trench?
Tosha, Gesha. Take everyone and drag those burdenbeast brains out here.
The positioning data show there are quite a few of those runaway Infected miners. Do you need our help?
How many heads are in your squad?
We're a small one. Just six of us.
That's a big group.
If you say so. We're the only two remaining idle squads around here, and we got here first.
So you have to share the spoils even if you don't want to.
What—
Arrrggghhh!!!
A shrill shriek breaks off the stale standoff.
Sounds of struggle and bones broken by blunt instruments immediately follow.
What's going on—
Help! Save me! They've gone mad—
Blyat! These Infected are all crazy!
You're not wrong...
You'll witness both the wrath and madness of the Infected.

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District Laboratory, Ursus

lines.x: Ugh...
Ksenia!
Ksenia, are you awake? How are you feeling?
Feeling? I don't know. Am I supposed to feel something?
You're not Kal'tsit.
No... you were never her, why did I even ask you that...? Suka, my head—what am I even saying?
I'm Mon3tr, an Operator of Rhodes Island... and also a companion to Kal'tsit.

Mon3tr, a familiar name, though I don't remember where I heard it... And what about you? Why are you—
*cough*, *cough* *cough*!
Easy now, no need to rush!

opts: I am Doctor.
We're from Rhodes Island.
We need your help.

lines.x: So you're indeed connected to Director Kal'tsit.
Rhodes Island... Serafina talked about the place. What sort of help do you need?
What help can I even give you, as I am now?

opts: Take this back.

lines.x: Oh, bog. Burn it.
I thought I'd be awake enough to destroy it myself... but it seems staying sober did not save me from this headache anyway.
This was written for the dead, and I hope it did not cause you any offense.

opts: Sorry, but we read it.
We have questions.
You were a Sarcophagus researcher in Chernobog.
Your writing and way of addressing Kal'tsit prove this as well.

lines.x: That was a long time ago...
I was just an assistant then, following Professor Ilia around and running basic data analyses. I barely had any significant ties to the project...
But now I see it is the very link to my ruin. I'd rather all of that had never happened at all.

opts: But you still treat Kal'tsit as your director, and the research is still on your mind.
Do you know where the Sarcophagus is currently located, or where it possibly could have gone?

lines.x: It's complicated to explain, but we really have to find the Sarcophagus, we—
No, wait. Explain. What do you mean by 'currently located'?
Is it not in Chernobog?
—What?
So, not only is the Sarcophagus no longer in Chernobog, the city itself has completely changed...
I don't know what happened, and I have no clue where it went either...
Sorry, I can't help you.

opts: Then, do you know of...
Artyom Chaadayev?

lines.x: You... how do you know this name?
Let's skip that first! You know him, right? Did he ever tell you anything about the Sarcophagus—
No... I've actually never met him.
You what...?
After the incident in Chernobog that year, someone with that name contacted me by mail...

He called himself a friend of Professor Ilia, expressed his condolences about what happened, and showed a willingness to help out a student of his old friend and recommend me to some other laboratories within the country.
I once thought it a nasty joke... until the well-funded labs mentioned in the letter actually came looking for me.
This would happen a few more times. Indeed he gave me many rare opportunities... but even now I have no idea who this man is, or why he helped me.

opts: He contacted you again recently.

lines.x: Yes... He invited me to Deity Grypherburg, claiming there was an important project I could deploy my skills on.
But I was tired, what could I even do...? Looking back on what I did, all I could bring was more calamity.
If I had to choose where to end my miserable life, let it be the frozen soil out here in the Far North.
(Doctor... what do we do now?)

opts: (That project in Grypherburg sounds somewhat suspicious.)
(It sounds like she doesn't know much about it either.)
Ksenia, how did you come to know Serafina?

lines.x: The lab had to supervise the mobile medical team in the mining district, so we got acquainted rather quickly.
Though we never spoke much to each other. They don't allow researchers to get too close to anyone unrelated to the project.
It's a bit too late now, if you wanted to find her. She left the lab with your people.
With that group of miners...
That's all I know.

opts: What is the 'Oka' mentioned in your writing?
What kind of experiments did you do on the Infected miners?

lines.x: A wretched mistake...
I didn't know... I had no idea they would use 'Oka' to do those things...

opts: Calm down...
There's still time to rectify mistakes.

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District, Ursus

lines.x: The Central Mining District is huge, and all eleven mines are connected by a surface transport system.
Stations are installed at every mine to deliver raw ore to be washed, processed, and stored in the warehouse.
The map seems to indicate this is the largest warehouse, and the worker dormitories are right next to it, with the south mine exit behind.
This layout...
Storing Originium in a warehouse so close to the dormitories does not mean they trust the miners, but that they absolutely do not care about the hazard posed by active Originium particles.
Amiya.
Yes? Did you find something?
A chip.
I saw it from the research lab terminal. Every miner here has a positioning chip in them. This could be why the miners we met earlier were found by the Patrol Unit.
Before the miners we were chasing chose to leave, they must have gotten rid of these.
Did they tear out the chips themselves?
(Shakes head)
You were right, Captain! In the south dorms, we found bandages, hemostats and other medical equipment, all Rhodes Island issue.
There's blood on the bandages and equipment. They turned the metal beds into a makeshift operating table, and might have performed surgery on it.
I suspect quite a big fight happened here, and the injured had to patch up their wounds.
So those are normal findings.
(Shakes head)
Captain's asking if you've seen any tracking chips that were forcibly removed.
No sign of any such thing.
No, we've swept this area.
As Eloysius says, the man-made damage inside and outside the warehouse seems to imply a fight broke out.
But I think it was a minor scuffle, unless the fresh snow is covering up some of the aftermath...
(Nods)
Alright, good work!
Captain, should we go take a look?
There is no answer, but the Phidia walks straight in the warehouse's direction.
The soldier pauses, then follows the group's footsteps.
Looks like you figured it out. To be honest, I called it a big fight, because—
The Guardswoman steps to one side and motions Elysium to help lift a waterproof sheet.
The revealed hole is filled with uneven matter covered up with a white substance that at a glance appears to be thick and dirty snow.
This smell... doesn't seem to be snow.
Correct. It's limestone.
And below it are corpses.
I told your Operator earlier I already investigated the area, and they took it as-is, and did not search the place any further.
I wasn't going to tell you about this...

They probably wanted to burn the bodies first, before realizing they couldn't get a big enough fire going and resorted to whatever was around. Quicklime to cover the stench, and the waterproof sheet to hide the hole.
It worked, since we didn't smell the blood, yes?
(Shakes head)
Captain's saying the dead are Patrol Unit members.
I can't say for sure. I'll climb down the hole and find out just who our unlucky customers are.
I... I'll help.
It's fine. Just pull me up later.
Eloysius jumps right down, and stands on the edge of the hole as she wipes some limestone off.
She grabs a sleeve, quickly drops it, and goes over to check the clothes on the next body.
Alright. Could you help me back up?
Before anyone extends their hand, Mantra moves her thick tail down and wraps it around Eloysius to hoist her up, then releases her gently onto the ground.
Ugh...
I've never been treated like this!
(Shakes head)
Alright not the best time for that so sorry Captain.
I'll get to the point. Most of the bodies down there are indeed Patrol Unit members—the first and second layers, at least.
I found this too.
The crowd looks at the fragile crystal fragment in her hand. Most of it has been charred, but the neat circuit proves it used to be a chip.
Quite a few on the ground down there. Fragments like this one, or even less.
It seems like they made a difficult choice.
You're putting it lightly.
(Shakes head)
Captain says, the miners probably attracted the Patrol Unit with their chips, and then offed them all in one shot.
After all, they only got the time to remove everyone else's chips after eliminating their pursuers.
This sort of organization and ability... certainly not your garden variety miners.
Their leader definitely has military expertise... or perhaps something else.
Something else...?
An Ursine folktale. 'The great Yurodstvo descends upon those in distress, and leads them out of dire straits'... just a tale born of boredom, though, so don't pay it much mind.
But we ought to find them before their actions become more radical.
After all, it's just the Patrol Unit today, but tomorrow it could be the military police, and then... it might just be the Royal Guard.
We have to find them before things get even worse.
Mantra... what are you looking for?
Mantra's eyes sweep through her surroundings, and after a long silence, Amiya hears her voice in her mind.
That young Imperial Guardswoman reminded me of something.
The Yurodstvo.
I think... I remember seeing this word in Dr. Kal'tsit's documents. It was related to Ursine research on demons...
A long, long, time ago, I too was on Ursine soil.
After a few mishaps, I was taken to be a Yurodstvo.
Only then did I learn of an unrecorded Ursus secret, so hidden that even libraries in Sargon's City of Gold did not know it.
The people of Ursus venerate the Yurodstvo; they believe the Yurodstvo's love transcends both the mortal realm and suffering, and this is how it has the power to lead one astray.
So you think what's happening here is related to the Yurodstvo? But shouldn't the Yurodstvo be at the heart of Deity Grypherburg...?
Only a hunch of mine. Everything that has happened here gives me a sense of unsettling familiarity.
Amiya follows Mantra's gaze and scans the area, but does not catch anything unusual.
Perhaps I was overthinking. But I also found some unexpected delight.
Raidian has left a beacon nearby.
News from her? Wonderful!
So this is how you and Raidian communicate—
(Nods)
Back at Babel, Raidian was my first assistant.
But her talent far exceeded that role, and she led her own squad after becoming an Elite Operator.
I heard there used to be many on Rhodes Island who feared you before Raidian joined us...
Occasionally, it proves to be true.
Some of our colleagues are better at speaking than thinking, and interactions can be problematic, while I am no expert at 'explanation.'
That is how my 'fearsome reputation' got passed around the Rhodes Island water coolers.

A few emergency operations where I probed into minds without prior notice, and after several rounds of exaggerated retellings, I became a witch who could stick my hand into their heads to toy with their minds.
Oh! I heard this one, in a story Theresa told me... I thought it was a Sarkaz legend.
Perhaps that was a true Sarkaz tale. The Speaker would have been defending me in saying that.
After Raidian joined Babel, she stood among those defending my reputation.
But today... Elysium is the flagbearer in that regard, as are you.
It's our responsibility!
(Nods)
Just as I thought.
Found them.
Mantra reaches out to wipe snow off the beacon's base, and slowly the symbols on it are revealed.
She feels the faint signals left on them, and some words gradually float into her mind.

lines.st: 'Ursus-Kazimierz Border'
'Reunion'

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District, Ursus Three days ago...

lines.x: You know what, doctor?
We beat those Patrol Unit monsters!
Please, calm down and don't move! It's a deep wound!
Haha! No big deal, my life would've been well-lived, even if all I managed was ending the lives of those brutes.
How can I even relax like this?
I never imagined this. Those high and mighty Patrol Unit guys couldn't either, I bet. That one day they would have to bow down to the Infected!
He even asked us to spare his life? Lucky I went straight for his head!
Rankin was right. No one can tread on us forever! We'll drag those old bastards down from their ivory towers one by one, and let them have a taste of the ground!
But...
*sigh*...
Rankin, we've already searched the place.
A total of ten Ursine medium-sized military trucks, with little fuel left. If everyone gets on, it'll only last us around two hundred kilometers.
Besides weapons and medicine, we found some food and water. It's not much, though it's no medicine either...
That's pretty good already.
I don't think these were worth the price we paid...
Right, we discovered a container door with extra encryption on it... we need an identification code.
Hmph... Can anyone here tell me what other secrets are lying around here?
Patrolling the mines at this hour, on this day? No telling me you stumbled here from boredom.
I don't know...
I'm just a driver! No better than a burdenbeast, I go wherever the captain tells me to!
The code or your life. Choose.
I don't know! I really don't know!
They've never given us the code to prevent the goods from running off! Only the recipient can open it!
'Running off'? What kind of 'thing' would do that?
Answer me! What the hell's inside those containers?
Nikto, give me one of those explosives.
Rankin, what are you—
Just do as I say!
Rankin sticks a small Originium bomb on the container's lock. A simple move, but he almost drops it from how fast he's breathing.
Ah...
The door slowly falls.
Rankin sees no fewer than thirty to forty bodies packed in a ten-meter-squared container, all long deceased, with no exception.
Some had struggled to the very last, their Originium crystals piercing into neighbors with messy, bloody wounds—no doubt, they were stuffed in here still alive.
Rankin can no longer tell what stole their lives away, and he does not wish to think about it...
Did you know?
I, what—
Drop the act!!
Who was it... who gave you the order?!
The mine boss—No, the Grand Duchess! She made us do it!
She cares about the miners here! Those in charge here get punished if the head count drops!
They always have to patch up the gap by buying some Infected with their own funds whenever one miner escapes—sometimes they get non-Infected to solve their problem, and it costs more.
But I have nothing to do with all that! Look, you already killed the actual bad guys!
I'm just a driver who listens to his boss... I'm the same as you, right...?
Infected, sir, please, let me go. I promise I'll desert the Patrol Unit, just pretend I died—
No, we're different, you and I.
None of us willingly became Infected... but you had a choice. You could have chosen not to stand on the same side as those murderers.
There's no reason I should forgive you.
Rankin, we're done organizing the food we found.
These Patrol Unit guys have it good. Tonight we can—
...Father?
...What?
Father? Is that really you?
Wonderful! Please, explain it to him! Tell him I'm not evil—My father's an Infected, how could I hate them?
Ganea... didn't you... go to Grypherburg...?
I did! I really did, I didn't lie to you!
But I needed money to stay alive, even in Grypherburg! They don't just hand out bread and mead—being a driver was the only option I had.
Father, please... save me... I'll never do this again...
Hawley...
He silently stares at the oblivious elderly miner, then gestures at Hawley with his head to look at the container.
I won't try to reform him, much less release him.
If he truly is your son...
Rankin throws a knife on the ground and walks away.
No... wait...
This, is not my boy, is it?
Father...?
Even the late autumn weather fails to prevent the corpses' rotting stench from spreading out into the air.
The old miner's gaze shifts between the mess of mangled limbs in the dark depths of the container and the tall young man before him...
Finally, he shakes his head incessantly, as if refusing to believe the truth.
My son wouldn't... wouldn't say such words. He wouldn't do something this disgraceful.
You're just... a damned bastard...
Why—Why do you wear my son's skin? Why do you live inside his body?!
Originally trembling, the old miner explodes into a rage, knocking the man down to the ground.
His fists rain like a storm, as if trying to drive something out of the body.
The man has no strength to resist.
Who are you? Who the hell are you?!

Oh, poor Ganea, his soul could have been saved! This isn't how it should have been! It was you, abomination, that left him with nowhere to go!
Just—
Die!!!
At least eat something, Hawley.
Look, white bread. You don't see it much outside the cities.
*sigh*...

lines.loc: Far North Tundra, UrsusThree days ago...

lines.x: Huh?
They've started singing...
Nikto! Found you at last.
Come on, Nikto, oddball, city-dweller, professor and cynic. Let's dance. It's just the right kind of music.
Now's not the time for dancing.
The bonfire is going to draw attention in this darkness.
But we found a tractor and a bunch of supplies. Not much, but enough to get us further away from the blasted place. Isn't that worth celebrating?
Unchipped, out of the mines, beat the Patrol Units, and now we have a tractor and supplies. It's beyond my wildest dreams.
Where are Vetochki and Rankin?
You're always looking for them. It's not like you're bound to each other. It's okay to give them a little breathing room, you know.
I do happen to know where Rankin is, though. Beside that bonfire that you don't like. Want to join us?
Last question. Who's playing the accordion?
You'll find out, if you come with!
Nikto takes the gloveless hand offered to him. The palm is rough but warm, different from any other in those memories that he has intentionally forgotten.
They walk in the direction of the bonfire and music.
He...
I said he was here.
Yes, he is here.
Many times Nikto saw him clench his hands into fists, or swing a weapon.
Nikto could not imagine how those hands, knotted with scars and thick with veins, could operate something as delicate as an accordion.
He plays the accordion?
Yeah. What about it?
By the fireside, Rankin's callused fingers leap across the keyboard, as he gently pulls and presses the bellows.
Music fills the air.
See? I told you.
Come, don't just stand there. It's not like we get to relax every day!
She catches him like a hunter catches prey. As he is brought into the crowd, his feet instinctively begin to step in rhythm.
Nikto was once an excellent dance partner. He tried to stop thinking about 'once' when he came to the mining district.
Get that frown off your brow and everything off your mind. Just dance, let your body move and rid yourself of all your troubles.
The crowd moves and sings to the beat, responding to the rhythm with every joint in their bodies.
It is the simplest, most primitive form of dance.
...Hah.
Nikto exhales and begins to move, bending his knees, kicking and spinning, every movement animated and rhythmic.
I knew it! You can dance!
See? I figured no one could deny the energy here.
Nikto does not answer, continuing to kick and turn mechanically, unemotionally.
Step forward and step back, jump and turn, until the next downbeat.
He takes the hands of his dance partner, and begins to lead the dance.
Wow—
No need to thank me.
No humility in you, is there? But...
It's clear that you're really good at dancing, even to an untrained eye. Your speed, and this 'magic' under your feet... it's almost like flying.
What do they call it? Pas? E-Enchainement?
Call it the pathetic instincts of an ornament.
What do you mean—
There is no explanation. Stepping energetically in time, Nikto pulls his partner towards him, then pushes her in the opposite direction.
Laughing, she runs into a young man without a partner, but eager to try, and so Nikto lets go and hands her over to her new partner.
By the time the dance has ended, Nikto is standing very near Rankin. Take one step back, and he would be sitting by Rankin's side.
But Nikto does not do so. Before he opens his mouth, he reminds himself: Do not press. Ask Rankin about the truth calmly, gently. Do not cause a commotion.
Do not interrupt this rare moment of joy.
I didn't know you played the accordion.
I didn't know you danced.
I guess we're not close enough to share everything.
But there is something you must tell me, if you really consider me a friend.
You went to see Hawley. Is he still not eating?
What did you expect?
We need to talk, Rankin...
Rankin sighs, brings the melody to a close, and hands the accordion to a nearby miner.
Nikto recognizes the face of Pavel, Bogina's brother. Is Bogina still running a fever? He does not know.
Pavel takes the accordion and begins to sing an unfamiliar song.
Lively, soaring, yet sad.
'Sweet soft fragile flower, lean against the oak tree...'
'Hang not your head, nor sway with the wind...'
(Hushed) Let's talk over there.
Rankin gets up, and they leave the crowd and commotion behind.
You didn't need to do that.
You could have done it yourself, like you usually did. I would have praised you for your kindness, if you had sent Hawley away on some sort of pretext when you did the deed.
But why did you make Hawley do it?
'Rankin the Noble.' 'Rankin the Hero.' Is it not enough that you've triumphed over the foe? Did you need to torture your own comrades like that?
Does it make you happy? Make your victory more glorious? Why did you—
Because I was angry.
Yes... I won't make excuses, pretend that I expected Hawley to let his son go. This was what I wanted. I'm happy about it.
Should I be calm? Should I be merciful? End his pain like you said? Why should I?
Did you think the people who were loaded into their containers did not beg for mercy? Beg for someone to end their pain?
You accuse me of being unkind. Who would accuse those beasts on their behalf?
I do not understand them. They look human and they speak the language of humans, but their deeds are more monstrous than any monster.
How could they prey upon their own kind like that, so calmly, so coldly?
This difference frightens me. It's as though... as though we never shared the same human dignity with them. If they're not the monsters... then it must be me who is the monster.
Rankin's words are clear, his tone impassive, as he gazes into the black night. Nikto wants to turn Rankin's head around and make him say it to Nikto's own face.
But he does nothing.
Silence.
But you were right. I didn't need to do that.
It's pointless...
You're torturing yourself, Rankin.
You shouldn't pretend to be as strong as you think you are, and you shouldn't demand that others be as strong.
It's not my place to accuse you. You carry the responsibility of everyone on your shoulders. You must become the hero. It's a remarkable thing.
But you can't pretend that the fear and hesitation in your heart don't exist... you're trying to hide from yourself.
The result is what happened today... you lost control. It's dangerous. You don't know what you might do, the next time your emotions get the better of you.
Thank you for the warning... I'll remember it.
Let's gather the others and put the bonfire out. It's time to go—how far until the next village?
...Let's wait a while longer.

They're happy. They haven't been so happy for a long time. We found a vehicle, and we're one step closer to the Kazimierzian border... let them enjoy themselves a little longer, and warm themselves up a bit.
You enjoyed yourself when you danced just now, didn't you?
What about Hawley? Do you still care about how he's feeling?
He won't eat anything, and he's not responding to people talking to him.
He'll come around.
You can keep thinking that way, if it makes you feel better.

Rankin turns around, takes a few steps towards the campfire, before stopping in his tracks. He turns back around and walks in the other direction, until he leaves behind the friend with whom he has developed differences, and the comrades still enjoying themselves.
He keeps walking until the scene of unfading crimson once again fills his thoughts.
What do I do, Rankin? I-I think my skull's crushed. There's blood everywhere—
Help me, Rankin! I don't want to die!
Rankin—
Sorry.
Accept it.
Accept pain as you accept mortal pleasures. Accept death as you accept the life that Ursus grants.
Therein lies salvation.
Yes, I accept.
Who are you? Who the hell are you?!
Our souls could have been saved! This isn't how it should have been! It was you, abomination, that set us adrift, never to find salvation!
It's your fault—your fault that we have nowhere to go—
Abandon all hope.
All of you...
You never had anywhere to go.
(Panting heavily)
*pant*... *pant*...
Nadia... why are you here? Is Vetochki not with you?
I came to find you... Rankin.
Why... is there something I can help with?
No... I would like to help you.
Oh...
You killed that thing. It became your revelation, to carry the burden of all.
I understand your pain... it is painful to carry the burden of the life and death of others, to carry the burden of duty... how could it not be?
I witnessed how your soul became torn and tattered. But I do not have the cure. I could not compliment your courage, for it was the source of your pain...
Thank you for your understanding.
But there is no need to pity me. I chose this duty for myself.
Besides... no, I never believed in revelations. I've always done what I thought was right.
Yes. You chose it yourself. Poor boy... no one can save you, except yourself...
I can only send you my sincere blessings, that one day... one day, you will find salvation from pain, and the peace that you desired...
The old woman puts a withered hand on Rankin's chest, and he feels his chest filled with warmth.
I—
Rankin touches his chest.
Was he too tired, or hallucinating?
He does not feel his heartbeat.

lines.st: Since you asked, let's start with the crackdown in the mining district.
It began with people who had too much time on their hands, who discovered a new substance deep within an exhausted mine shaft.
Of course, we began to study it.

lines.x: The Infected are one thing, but why can't we leave the mining district either?
There's nothing coming out of the mines. The miners are being shut inside their quarters, unable to go to work. Who knows if the Originium veins inside have really dried up?
Let's sit and wait. The Grand Duchess won't abandon us.
Wait? What's the point of waiting? They're not issuing our salaries, just supplies. Why not just let us leave, then? Not everyone's so carefree like you. I've got a mother to provide for.
We've got news! The mines haven't been exhausted!
What? Really?
Guess what we found at the bottom of the mine?
The Originium's grown back?
No! You don't have any imagination.
Imagination leads to futile hope, which leads to death. I don't want to die yet.
So, what exactly did you find?
Geologists from Bresk say that it's a geological fault, not exhaustion! Faults cause shifts in rock formation, resulting in Originium ore seemingly disappearing, when in fact it's been transformed.
Can you explain it in a way that a non-geologist could understand?
Meaning that the Originium ore is gone, but something new has appeared.
Look at this! Fresh out of the hole. I'm taking it to the lab for analysis. Here's your chance to help!
The excited researcher takes out a flask of black liquid, the surface of which is smooth as a mirror, and shakes it gently.
Are you sure? I'm no geologist, but I don't think faults turn rock from solid to liquid...
Are you sure they didn't make it up just so that we'll have something to do?
I went down myself, alright? The stuff's everywhere. It's like an underground river. The miners said the Originium ore was gone, but it's only been transformed into this stuff.
You have a chance to be a co-author on my paper! You should be more grateful!
Fine, fine I'll come. Like I don't know you just wanted to use my lab.
And what about it? Yours is the tidiest...

lines.st: Indeed. How could a fault turn Originium ore from solid to liquid?

But there was no need for us to doubt the truth that was plain as day. After swift analysis and comparing the results, we confirmed that it was a substance the likes of which we've never seen before, and we named it 'Oka'.
Then, the Grand Duchess' orders came.

lines.x: Go straight into human testing? Who ordered it, the director?
No, not even on the director's orders. We don't understand its nature, and we haven't even begun animal testing or energy testing. We're being too hasty. I'm going to find him—
There's no point talking to the director. The volunteer quota has already been filled, and Lakoda is moving forward with the tests.
Besides, this comes directly from the Grand Duchess. She wants results in two weeks.
Two weeks? That's not even enough to find out its half-life.
I don't like this. I—
If you don't like it, you can leave the Oka research.
I was in charge of extracting the active component of Oka, and I worked with Lakoda to develop the animal testing reagent. Of course I want to continue!
I just—I just don't think this is right, to go straight to human testing with zero idea about what might happen.
The sooner we get results, the sooner the Grand Duchess lets me go home. That's my only concern right now.
Like I told you, not everyone is as carefree as you are.
But—

lines.st: The human experiments began regardless. I was excluded and did not have access to the experiment data.
I wanted to figure out the nature of Oka and why it appeared in the mines. So...
The experiments on lab musbeasts showed no progress. At my wits' end, I even thought about experimenting on myself.
I never did, of course, because I learned that those so-called volunteers were not volunteers after all.

lines.x: You need to get out of here, Ksenia.
Serafina? How did you get in here? I thought there were guards at the door. Are there new volunteers from the miners—
Listen to me, Ksenia. Those miners never volunteered!
Even the patients who were in our medical post were forcibly conscripted. Your colleague was such a smooth liar that I didn't detect she was lying at first, when she said they volunteered.
What?
All those 'volunteers' who participated in the experiments never volunteered! They never came back! Whatever's happening here goes against all scientific ethics.
You remember Vetochki? The bell toller, the one who stammers a little?
Good girl. She remembers everyone in the mining district, and she told Rankin, the foreman, as soon as she noticed that people had gone missing.
First, the Infected miners, then their non-Infected families...
Rankin went missing for a while too, reappearing with the bodies of a few miners who had gone missing.
Gosudar-batyushka... so when Lakoda said that the experiments were paused because of 'equipment theft'...
I don't know the details about your situation, but the miners have left their quarters and are coming to the lab. They want an explanation.
The miners are mostly Infected, and they have the strength of numbers. The guards can't stop them if they're determined to force the issue. There will be blood.
But... but I've never heard about any volunteers showing rejection symptoms, much less death—
Are you sure?
I... no, I'm not.
Do you have the experiment data?
No. I've been looking for the director all day to request access, but I haven't been able to find him.
I don't know what you're researching here. Ultimately, the worst that can happen to the Infected is to be incinerated.
If this place still retains the function of a hospital, as His Imperial Majesty hoped, you can check the incinerator or morgue.
After that, you can come to the medical team's camp if you want to come with me.

lines.st: I didn't accept Serafina's invitation.
But she was right.

Clothing without owners was stacked haphazardly in the basement. The incinerator was still running. The last person keeping watch over the place had left, leaving behind bodies that had not yet disintegrated scattered like the clothes.
The volunteers who participated in the experiment died en masse in the span of just two weeks. The impact of Oka on the human body was beyond doubt.

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District Laboratory, Ursus

lines.x: As was the case with the human experiments, I was excluded from the evacuation.
The lab was empty by the time I went to the basement, learned the truth, and put all the still-intact bodies in cold storage.
In fact, I can only guess that there was an evacuation. I don't know where they went—the director, Lakoda, or anyone else.
Thinking back on it, it was just like Chernobog, except this time I was the one in the lab.
You were too young at the time, and you were on the project as a student intern. You were not on the list of core staff.
H-How did you know?
Were you—

opts: I understand.
We found 'Dominion' pollution in the mining district.

lines.x: The Royal Guard was here. Not anymore.

opts: Before you passed out, you said that Oka could kill a Royal Guard.
Then you said the miners killed a Royal Guard.
Which one is true? Or are they both true?

lines.x: (Mutters) ...Chyort.
I don't think there's anything to talk about.
How does an ordinary person who was born and raised in Ursus describe the sudden collapse of the violence that had dominated her life for so long?
The Royal Guards are the stuff of nightmares to the children of Ursus. They watch your every word and action, so you are cautious and careful, so you are well-behaved and obedient.
They are invincible, undefeatable.
When you suddenly witness the death of one... it only means that something even more terrible has appeared.

opts: The miners touched by Oka killed the Royal Guard.

lines.x: No, no... I don't know anything about it. Forget I ever said anything.
I didn't see anything, and you didn't hear anything. Just leave.
I've told you everything you wanted to know, everything that I can tell you.

opts: Even if all it does is accelerate the deaths of the Infected...
It would be an unimaginable disaster if it got out.
Leave aside the question of who killed the Royal Guard, or even if anyone killed him at all.
We don't know if the fleeing Infected miners came into contact with the substance.

lines.x: If they did, and it is contagious, they would not have allowed the researchers to leave.
The changes brought about by Oka are not necessarily contagious. At least, based on the experiment data that I managed to find, they haven't begun comparative observation yet—

opts: Do you still have a sample of Oka?

lines.x: No... What would you intend to do with it?

opts: It's not about what we intend to do. It's about what we can do.
We'll have to get our hands on a sample to find out.

lines.x: But all the samples are... you're going into the mines?! Now?

opts: It seems to be the only option.
Don't you want to find out what Oka really is?

lines.loc: Central Far North Mining District, Ursus

opts: Do you need a break?

lines.x: Huh? Oh... uh, no.
I just...

opts: If you have reservations...
We can look for it ourselves.

lines.x: (Shakes head)
I chose to be your guide. Besides, I want to know the truth about Oka too.
Worst case scenario, I guess they exile me...
The path ahead seems to be safe, Doctor. I do not detect any lifeforms—
Huh?
Ksenia glances at Mon3tr, then quickly and awkwardly looks away.
You notice the researcher has been following Mon3tr quietly, looking to her from time to time. Even Mon3tr has noticed it now.
You keep glancing at me, Ksenia... do I look weird?
What? No, no!
It's just, seeing you brings back memories.
What memories?
You see... the problem is that I don't remember them clearly.
It's so distant that it's become faded and indistinct, like ink on a piece of paper that's been submerged in water...
But... may I ask you a couple of questions?
Of course! I only hope I can answer them.
You said your name is Mon3tr?
Yes, that's what they call me.
Mon3tr... Mon3tr.
She chews on the unfamiliar name.
Mon3tr, do you—
Do you feel cold...?
Huh?
Wh-What I mean is, you're quite lightly dressed.
I can give you my jacket, if you need it. It'll at least keep the wind off you before we enter the mines.
I guess it might be a little too old—
I-I get it! There's no need!
It took me a little while to process it because you're the first one to ask me this question. But no, I'm really not cold!

opts: (Shake head at Mon3tr)
(Nod to Ksenia)

lines.x: No, I'm not cold. But thank you for the offer!
Were you about to say something, Doctor?

opts: No, you two can continue.

lines.x: Ah... alright.
Perhaps your constitution is strong, and the cold doesn't bother you.
Yes! I'm strong!
It's alright, Ksenia. You can trust us.
We'll be walking together for a while. Feel free to ask any questions.
Right...
You said you were colleagues of Director Kal'tsit...
Is... is Director Kal'tsit well?
Um...

opts: Were you close with her?

lines.x: I... I wouldn't say that.
I was Professor Ilia's assistant. I didn't have much interaction with Director Kal'tsit personally.
All I remember is a few...
Ksenia! Ksenia!
Y-Yes, Professor!
Why isn't the experiment data from this morning on my desk?
I'm sorry! I'll go get it right now!

How many times do I have to tell you? You need to be able to keep up with the pacing, if you want to be part of the project. If all I wanted was someone to copy instrument readings onto paper, my three-year-old daughter could do it.
And what are these scribbles? Energy conversion equations?
Do your job first, Ksenia, before you try anything new.
What is this?
D-Director Kal'tsit. It's nothing, nothing to waste your time on.
Ksenia is still young. It'll take time for her to get used to working on an active project. I'll take responsibility for any mistakes...
Did you come up with this equation yourself?
Yes...
The theoretical model is quite creative, and the simulation model is sound. The equation would form the basis of highly efficient power supplies, if it can be successfully established.
Kinetic energy loss has not been taken into account, however, and more validation of the data is necessary.
Good grief...
I... I'm sorry, Director Kal'tsit.
There's no need to apologize. You took initiative, you wanted to be a part of what's going on.
You can listen in on the next report session, if you want.
R-Really?
And don't be so hard on her, Ilia. One should encourage the enthusiasm of youth appropriately.
You made worse mistakes when you were her age.
You're right. I understand.
Th-Thank you...
It's true that Director Kal'tsit inspired me at one point.
Like many who were there, I had hoped that research into the Sarcophagus would yield a new form of clean energy that could benefit everyone.
That way, miners would be liberated from the dangers of the mines, and the lives of the Infected would no longer be needed to fuel this monstrosity by the name of Ursus.
I was young, naive... it never occurred to me that a country's character, like the character of a person, is not so easily shaken or changed.
Regardless, I was very happy I got to hear Director Kal'tsit's name after the accident.
I thought, maybe I would have a chance to...
See her again?

opts: About that, Ksenia...

lines.x: Sorry! Forget I ever said anything. I assume it's something you may not answer.
I just thought... you look like you might know the Director. Perhaps you knew her, knew where she went, and if she's doing well.
But I suppose it was never my place to know...
Ksenia.
Yes?
You'll see Kal'tsit again. I promise.
Oh, so you mean she is still...
I am delighted to hear that.
We'll see Kal'tsit again, won't we, Doctor?

opts: ...Yes.

lines.x: I look forward to it...
Should I get the chance, I would like to go to this 'Rhodes Island' that Director Kal'tsit founded, and which Serafina has mentioned...
One moment... something's not right...
We didn't see anyone on the way, did we?
No miners, and even though we're very near the mine shafts, we haven't seen a single guard, a single Patrol Unit.

opts: The Grand Duchess put the entire mining district on lockdown.
Was everyone transferred?

lines.x: I know about the lockdown. But the transfer—
No... What I know is completely different.
I thought the only problem was the failure of the Oka experiments. I never heard anything about the mining district being cleaned up.

opts: It's definitely strange.
We've hardly encountered any resistance since we entered the district.
The defenses are even sparser than inside Bresk.
The missing miners...
Where did they go?

lines.loc: Far North Tundra, Ursus

lines.x: Rankin puts the finishing touches by carving on the door of the carriage, then throws away the rock.
I know how to draw the symbol now. I'll help next time.
Sure. You can do it next time.
I'm done over here. Have we gotten the stuff out of the other containers?
Pavel's side is done. The last two containers didn't have much in the way of useful supplies. Should we remove the mechanical components and take them with us?
You said you used to work in the machine factories in the city?
Yes.
Take a few parts that can be used on other machines. Maybe we can sell them for money.
Assuming anyone would buy them...
We must also take the injured into account.
Gospozha Serafina.
We have too many injured. It's not realistic to immediately continue the journey on foot.
We need rest.
Rankin! We took inventory and we should have enough food to last two or three days if we ration it. It should last us until the next village.
I thought there would be more... but I suppose we should be glad to have what we have.
Vetochki found some nuts stockpiled by musbeasts. It'll sate the hunger for a while, and it doesn't spoil easily.
Right now, we need medical supplies more than we need food—
Where do we find it?
Only way is the nomadic cities, or a mobile medical post. If we can contact one...
Rankin looks towards Raidian.
I can attempt to contact a nearby mobile medical post.
Thank you, Gospozha Serafina.
Don't thank me so soon. I have a condition.
We appreciate what you and your team have done for us, but in our current circumstances—
The injured must stay put while I attempt to contact the mobile medical posts.
It's my judgment as a medical professional that they need rest.
The kind of rest that isn't bumpy.
Who are you taking?
Lidiya, Pavel, and a few sweet talkers who could coax coins out of their supervisors.
You didn't like those slick talkers from the ore-washing plant.
It has nothing to do with my preferences.
Why not me? Because I might argue with you?
We're seeking help from a nearby village, not soliciting donations from a noble banquet in Grypherburg, barin. Your way won't work with farmers who can't even write their own name.
Call me that again, and I'll break your nose.
You'll break your knuckles on it.
You don't think seeking help from the village is a good idea.
...Right.
Until Gospozha Serafina makes contact with a mobile medical post, however, we can't sit around doing nothing.
We're not injured.
We've sat around doing nothing. We sang and danced—you played the accordion!
All past tense. Our supplies will run out soon. I must do what I can before that happens.
While we're gone... I need you to stay here and take care of those staying behind.
I doubt the villagers would be charitable this time.
Of course. Few people are glad to open their door and see an Infected standing in it. We've already learned that.
How long has Rankin been in there? Should we check it out?
Calm down. Rankin told us to wait.
But last time—
Come out, Vetochki. I know you were following us. Rankin must have known, too. He just chose not to say anything.
Why didn't you stay behind with Nikto and the others? You need to take care of your babushka, don't you?
Babushka said, it's alright...
I wanted to, help. So I, came. I can, h-help!
Did Nikto know that you came with us? He might have thought that you went missing.
I left, a... note.
That works. You know, I thought you could be useful with us, but Rankin wouldn't have it.
Wh-Where has, uchitel, gone?
He's going to negotiate with them, see if we can get some food.
Wh-What do we, have to t-trade?
Parts from the vehicles.
But he went over empty-handed!
Maybe he wanted to show that he means no harm. People might get the wrong idea if he was holding a weapon or a chunk of metal.
But he didn't take anything the last time either, and those people stalled for time while they sneaked someone out to call for the Patrol Units—
I have to help him!
Pavel!
Chyort... wait up!
(Hushed) Stop it!
Rankin is inside—
The miner covers her colleague's mouth with her hand and pulls him to one side.
They can hear the indistinct sound of talking coming from inside the house.
...Understood...
(Sound of metal grinding)
...We've... informed...
(Sound of impact)
(Whisper) Something doesn't sound right.
Mmmpppphhh—
(Whisper) Promise you won't just kick down the door, and I'll let go.
(Nods)
As soon as he can breathe again, the miner points towards the back door of the house.
There, clear as day on the snow, is a row of footprints leading away from the house.
(Whisper) ...Govno.
(Whisper) One of you, go knock on the door. Act anxious and spin a story when they answer the door.
(Whisper) Why don't YOU go? If they've got Rankin, whoever knocks on the door is next, isn't it?
(Whisper) I-I'll go.
(Whisper) No, Vetochki—
Vetochki taps lightly on the door with her knuckles.
The sound from inside ceases.
Who's there?
(Whisper) ...Keep knocking.
(Knocks)
Back so soon...
Argh!
What's going on—
Sir!
Are you alright, Rankin?
Lidiya? I thought I told you to wait outside—
...Vetochki?
What are you doing here?
So you're all miners who came out under orders to find supplies.
Yes, sir! I must apologize for what that fool did. He spends so much time underground that he's forgotten how to tell a good person from a bad one.
I'm sorry, I'm so sorry. We had a bad experience when we tried to seek help from another village, and I thought this would be the same—
Pavel.
You're honest about things. I have a fair idea how people from other villages treat the Infected... most of Ursus is like that.
But you're not those Infected that don't care about authority and just want to cause trouble. You're miners, right?
Yes! You know, of course, that we can't leave the mining district without permission from up high.
This is our foreman right here. They told him not to go back until he's found supplies.
Would the bosses leave you to starve if you don't find it?
Yes. It's not so bad for adults like us, but kids like Vetochki over here, how are they supposed to grow without eating?
Vetochki? This girl? She's a miner too?
How old are you...?
I-I'm—
Let me guess. Ten?
Fifteen.
Haha. You're shy, like my daughter... she might have a child your age now, if she's still alive.
She went to the mines after she got the Stone Disease. Not around these parts, to the east, the Northern Tundra? It's further away, anyhow.
Th-The mines, are good. D-Don't worry... dedushka.
Dedushka... grandpa, huh...?
I don't even know how and when she got sick. She wouldn't say. I haven't heard from her for a long time.

Let's not talk about that! Sounds like you have people running your mining district. Some places don't care about their miners at all, giving them nothing to eat, and they don't give them any other way out either.
I heard some miners got so desperate they ran away. They'd rather risk the Patrol Units.
Can you help us?
My wife went out to discuss things with the others before you came in.
So those footprints were...
You have the parts, Lidiya?
Right here.
Rankin takes the cloth package and gives it to the old man.
Here, you should be able to replace the aging parts on your harvester with it.
In exchange for the food you shared with us.
Sure, sure... honestly, I don't know anything about harvesters other than that it's been broken for a while. Do you mind helping me fit the part on if you want to help?
I'll take a look. I used to work in a machine factory.
That's great.
You really managed to get food. A fair bit of it, too.

Gennady says it was a good year for crops, but his harvester was broken. They managed to harvest the mossoat by hand, only to have surplus grain sitting around in granaries because the caravans didn't come this year.
Life is hard for everyone without cash. Still, they were willing to barter with us, rather than waiting until spring to sell.
Lidiya said those parts are worth more than mossoat.
As far as we're concerned, food is priceless.
Must you go so soon?
Yes. We've accomplished our mission. It's time to go back.
I had hoped that you would fill your bellies before you go.

If you want, there are a few houses on the west end of the village that have been empty since the previous inhabitants left. Your group can take shelter there, make a fire, get warm and make some food.
Spasibo...
I can't tell you how grateful I am.
There's no need. The girl helped us repair the harvester for next year. We're the ones who should thank you.
Besides, it's not like we did much.
Normally, we don't touch the collective food stores for winter. Patrol Units are less frequent these days, but it's a chore when the tax collectors come around.
All we old folks did was to take a little grain out of our own reserves, so that you can go back and say you've done your job.
Not like there's anything else we can help with—
Maybe you can tell us your daughter's name. We can try to ask when we—uh, return to the mining district.
Oh, she's not here in the Far North! She's in the Northern Tundra, far to the east...
Just tell me.
Well, I suppose it can do no harm.
Her name is Milana...
A pair of blank, soulless eyes gaze out from the darkness.
The cracking of the bonfire hides his indistinct mutter.
Achieve...
Salvation.
No... no...
Argh!
The young woman falls on the ground before your eyes, a black spear 'growing' out of her body.
Next to her body, a man is flailing with his legs, trying to push himself away from this dangerous place.
M-Monster...!
Black is closing in. Black, black, black, everything is black, except for bright-red blood. You cannot even tell if you are facing something that is living or not.
Do not fear—not one step back!
Stand up, if you still have strength—
Ahh!!
The black steps through the bodies, some of whom were miners, some of whom were guards, and some of whom were just ordinary Ursines.
The constant screams make it very clear that, whether you turn to run or not, the spears appearing out of thin air will catch you.
You can only watch as the spears continue to rise from the ground, impaling body after body, threatening to blanket the entire ground.
Suddenly, the spears remove themselves from the mountain of corpses, forming a longsword in the hand of the black shadow.
The entity raises its sword and points it toward you, speaking in an indistinguishable voice.
You would die standing.
No... I'm not going to die...
I'm not going to die here.
Hope. It's a good thing.
But futile.
Is that...?
Rankin! We're here to help!
Rankin—
You—Renat?
We're here to help!
You're here! You're here to help me! My comrades, you're here—
We'll fight side-by-side, just like old times!
Just like old times!
Surround him! Just like old times! One falls, another takes his place!
Follow me!
Fool...
You feel the heat coming from behind you, like a burning fire that spreads all over you. It makes your blood run hot, and your eyes wide and clear. They shout your name and push you forward.
In the embrace of solidarity, you leap and take aim at what seems to be the thorax of the black entity—
And impale it.
—Death—
Ha...
There is no sound of blood dripping, no convulsion.
Only the resistance of the blade growing lesser and lesser, until it disappears altogether.
I see... I see...
...Gabrielus—
As the last syllable leaves its mouth, the uniform propped up by the shadow goes limp and stops resisting.
You have put an end to the monster, with courage, with determination, with heart.
With the support of 'friends' who never existed.

lines.st: *pant*... *pant*...
Your hands are heavy. Where are you?

lines.x: '—He's down. He's down!'

lines.st: ...Who?

lines.x: 'Rankin, you—we, we killed—'
'We killed him!'

lines.st: Killed who? Black... snow...
Not enough. One obstacle dead and gone, but the snow is everywhere, abominable snow...
The snow will not stop.

lines.x: 'Rankin? Rankin! What do we do now?'
'Rankin—'
Rankin!
I am...
Rankin! Rankin!
The crowd rushes toward you like the tide. They raise you up, and they pull you back down. You have felt this sense of weightlessness before.
The crowd shouts, the crowd yells. Someone who should not have been here shouts your name in the deafening echoes.
—Rankin—
You close your eyes and go back into the forest with his words. A border garrison unit, a one-way mission.
You are not there.
Won...
We won.
As you rise and fall, you look towards the black dust in the distance, which has yet to fully disperse.
Black smoke seeps out from every hole and gap in the uniform, like countless tiny tentacles, greedily wrapping and covering it, until it is completely hidden.
Under the black smoke, the uniform once again takes human shape, as though—
As though he never died.
The nights of deep autumn are cold in Ursus. Snow falls from the branches as the cold winds blow.
