The crowd has already dispersed. There is no light coming from the distant huts.
A tiny flicker of flame still burns on the charred firewood in the middle of the clearing, clinging to its last gasp of life as the north winds tear at it.
A piece of thick firewood is thrown into the pile, and sparks fly in the air.
They...
They found us.
Dry firewood lies in ash, slowly becoming charred and twisted, before being swallowed by the leaping flames.
The fire shines upon the face of the feeble Infected, but he feels no warmth.
The people here... everyone.
They must be wiped away... like Ganea was...
This way, they will never...
They will never lose their goodness, nor abandon purity...
Their souls shall never be defiled.
He is in pain, a pain that penetrates his heart like a needle.
He is cold, shivering like he just fell into a frozen lake.
He must make himself warm again.
One piece, two pieces, three pieces.
One bundle, two bundles, three bundles.
The flame that was so faint now pokes its head from the gaps in the wood like a burning demon, threatening to touch its creator with its tongue.
The frail miner picks up a burning firewood and walks toward the silent houses.
Tap, click, tap, click.
The footsteps are rhythmic, one stronger than the other.
Asleep... asleep!
They're all asleep.
You do not know what desperation awaits you, what judgment you will face.
Aiding and abetting the Infected before selling them out. Blissful ignorance, docile as a burdenbeast!
But it is alright.
I will help you. I can help you.
For I shall meditate...
And share...
In your pain.
The madman carries the torch of fear, but the flame can no longer stem the cold.
The cold pierces his bones. It comes from Oripathy, from the wind, from Ursus.
He needs warmth.
He sees a large pile of hay.
Fire.
He throws the torch.
Cleansing—
The flames awaken all that has withered.
Salvation!
He has never felt so acutely alive.
The ringing continues in his head. In the nightmare, Rankin is clinging to the last vestige of cognition and sanity.
His sensations are returning, but the wailing grown nearer, not more distant.
He forces himself up and staggers towards the door of the tent, seeking a breath of fresh air.
Hot air rushes at him, evaporating the sweat in an instant.
In the blink of an eye, all the feelings return to his body.
This is not a dream. It is real. The stuff of his nightmares has found a foothold in reality.
Everywhere, people are fleeing, as the houses that once offered refuge are devoured by fire.
In the gushing smoke, the scent of charred despair clutches at everyone's throats like a demonic claw. How tiny is man in the presence of terror.
Rankin!
What... what happened?
No idea. The fire started all of a sudden. We can't put it out!
All we can do is evacuate the others!
The food we traded for is still inside. I have to go in—
Now is not the time!
Wake everyone! Get them out of the village! Go!
But his commands do not bring order to the crowd. Fear continues to spread unchecked.
He has never...
Died.
No, no... I won! I killed him!
I can save everyone...!
Uchitel!
What are you doing here, Vetochki? Get to safety!
Uchitel, I-I...!
Didn't you hear me?!
Rankin tries to shake off Vetochki's hands, but the girl grabs his arm with all her strength.
B-Babushka, sh-she's gone!
What? Wasn't she with you?
The stammering girl gestures furiously, trying to explain what happened, but the terror on her face says it all.
Nikto!
Continue the evacuation! We'll go find Nadia!
O-Okay!
Rankin and Vetochki dart through the fire. The houses on both sides of them are burning, and the slightest misstep could set their clothes on fire.
Babushka... Babushka!
Rankin wants to yell too, but the feeling of helplessness continues to accumulate in his heart. It is stuck in his throat, preventing him from making a sound, preventing him from breathing.
He knows full well what a fire like this means for an invalid old woman.
Uchitel, I... you... mm!
At the smoke-filled intersection, Vetochki covers her mouth and points to herself, then at Rankin, and finally towards the fork in the path.
No, Vetochki... you must not go off on your own... listen to me...
Uchitel!
The girl's face is red as tears flow from her eyes. She holds her hands together in a strange gesture of prayer.
She is not praying to the Tsar, nor to the Yurodstvo. She is praying to Rankin.

lines.st: Fool.

lines.x: Ugh...!
A shadow flashes across their vision. He is limping, but there is an unusual strength in his steps, as though he is being drawn in by something.
Hawley? Where are you going?!
The hunched figure ignores Rankin's cries, walking towards the sea of flames at the end of the path.
Surrounded by the bright light of fire, his figure casts no shadow. He prepares to welcome baptism by fire, like the Yurodstvo walking towards the execution grounds in legend.
Vetochki! Wait!
I have committed murder.
Not for food, nor for water, nor for abode.
He continues to walk as he chants, and pushes open a burning door.
Even the scorching light of the flames cannot stop Rankin from seeing what is inside the house, over Hawley's head—
Flames roil and writhe as charred beams totter, cracking like the moaning of the dying.
An old woman sits straight in the middle of it all.
She is sitting on the last chair without a sound. The flames are only inches from her, but they cannot touch her, as though an invisible wall is keeping them at bay.
She has not a speck of ash or dust on her. Even her hair is immaculately clean. Her eyes gently shut, but at the same time she seems to be watching everything.
I have cleansed their souls.

lines.st: Snow, snow, snow.
Snow is falling on his head.
It turns into boiling water, into endless cries, into the weight of the dead.
Hands are not enough to sweep it away. It is wet, heavy, sinking...
Until it crushes those who are inside, those who are still living.

lines.x: (Humming)
Babushka, h-how are you feeling?
(Humming)
...Tick, tock.
Tick, tock.
(Humming)
The clock, ticks, tocks.
The clock, doesn't wait, for anyone.
But we, shouldn't sing?
Let's... l-let's go.
No more survivors here.
I don't think I have the strength to stand. Are you there, Lidiya? I could use a hand—
Lidiya?
Over there.
Someone's coming.
Rankin...
The fire's gone out.
Yes, it went out a while ago. Some survivors found us. We must do something.
Are you going to make Hawley kneel here forever? Even if he went mad and did all this, is it really necessary to punish him this way?
The fire's gone out, and it's getting cold. He'll die.
Yes, he'll die. But...
Not yet.
Rankin looks toward the entrance to the village.
The fence that used to be there was designed to stop foraging tuskbeasts, not anyone determined enough to enter, whether their intentions were benevolent, or malicious.
Scattered figures begin to emerge from behind the remains of the fence.
They look this way hesitantly, then walk away timidly.
Rankin sounds almost like he is muttering to himself—
I was wrong, Nikto.
What do you mean?
I was wrong.
In what way?
Hawley wasn't mad. He wasn't shouting for Vetochki's babushka, or to his dead son. He was shouting at me.
'...I have committed murder, not for food, nor for water, nor for abode.'
'But for those who walk with me, for the salvation of their souls.'
I didn't hear anything. Even if he did say it, it's just the kind of prayer that Vetochki and her babushka pray all the time.
It's not like they started worshipping the Yurodstvo and the Tsar yesterday.
Yurodstvo? Tsar? No, not distant or ethereal things like that. He was trying to save my soul, even after I made him kill his own son.
But what is a soul, even? Who can prove that it exists? Is it more precious than mortal life? It's ridiculous... silly!
I shouldn't have forced him. But now, the worst has happened. If he did what he did before in order to fulfill my wishes, then it was for the same reason that he set this fire.
No, it's nonsense, listen to me—
It might as well have been me who set this fire.
I must pay for my crimes.
Don't be a fool, Rankin!
I'll keep this brief.
What... what else do you have to say?
We gave you food and water.
Is THIS how you repay us?
No.
Are you here to chase us away, or to finish what you started?
I want you to witness the perpetrator face justice.

Ha... haha...
Hahahahaha...
Perpetrator? Justice? Will you kill all the arsonists, killers, and robbers in front of me?
No, you won't. Otherwise, you wouldn't be coming to talk to me while armed.
I'll pretend I didn't hear anything. Leave, unless you're trying to wipe us out.
Never come back again.
Rankin puts down his pickaxe and all other weapons, then raises both hands in the air.
Rankin!
Stay back, Nikto.
You still have your Arts...
Right here. The culprit is right here. You can do what you wish with him.
What?
I've put down my weapon. I won't use any Arts.
You can do what you wish.
I see. You would shield the others with your own life.
I've never been good with numbers... but I can tell that's not a fair deal.
I'm right here. I won't fight back.
I don't want you! We don't want you! You weren't here when the fire started!
I want the one who set the fire, the one who robbed my home and burned it down—
I want the one who killed my wife!
Even if I was the reason for his violence?
It was my arrogance that put the others in a desperate situation. It was I who forced him down the path of violence.
You should kill me.
No.
Listen to me—
We want him. We don't want anyone else.
A small space has been cleared in the rubble to serve as an impromptu execution ground.
A crowd has gathered to watch. It is as though they cannot help themselves, like huddling around a fire in the winter night, yet fearful that its warmth might burn.
I still don't understand. Why did Hawley set the fire?
Why would he do this to a village that helped us?
He's mad.
He was never the same again after his son died.
But—maybe he didn't set the fire? Rankin wanted to satisfy the villagers, and Hawley happened to be sick.
The Hawley I knew would never do something like that...
I don't know, but I trust Rankin. Didn't you know? He even tried to trade his life for Hawley's.
Thankfully, the villagers wanted the real culprit, not a good person trying to take his place.
...It hasn't started yet, but I can't watch...
I-I'll be over there. I need some rest.
Stop, Rankin's talking.
Pretty much everyone's here.
(Mutters) ...share...
(Mutters) Pain...
I have a few words to say before I execute this man. I'm not trying to defend him, or seek forgiveness from the victims.
Yes, he deserves to die.
He betrayed kindness and trust. He placed all of us in danger. He harmed the innocent, in direct violation of our original intent, which was simply to survive.
Death may be inevitable on the path we chose, but it should never have happened this way.
Watch. Remember the lesson, and remember that the one about to take the life of this man is the one least qualified to do so.
I am executing him, on the wishes of the victims, and on my own decision.
This will be the last decision I make as leader. No one should bear the burden of killing his own comrade. Therefore...
Repent...
Sin.
The snow has piled up on Hawley's shoulder. He kneels on the ground, as though he did not hear any of what Rankin said, and puts his hands together in front of his chest in a gesture of piety.
He looks up, and exposes his neck to the executioner.
Yes... I must repent my sins. I shall do the deed.
(Whisper) Do vstrechi, Hawley.
Gah!
Damn... I thought I could at least put this mirror together. Now it's gotten even more broken.
Talk about unlucky.
Can't we at least take Hawley's body, Rankin?
No.
What use do they have for a dead body? Do they want to leave it out in the wilds...
We're not in any position to negotiate.
Besides, all of you may come to the same end if you continue to follow me.
What do you mean?
Who do we follow, if not you? Now's not the time for jokes.
It's not a joke. It's a decision.
A decision that I already talked about.
Rankin turns around. The tired, unkempt crowd immediately gather around him, simply because he is here.
They look towards their leader, blankly waiting for the next instruction.
Do we keep going south, Rankin?
I don't have any strength left. I just wanted a roof over my head that could keep the snow away, but we don't even have that anymore, do we?
We'll die if we keep going...
We'll die if we stay.
I couldn't find them, Rankin.
Pavel and Bogina... I never found them, not even their bodies. I'm sorry.
The villagers didn't want me anywhere near them, not even when I put down my pickaxe. They're afraid. You could see it on their faces from a mile away.
I didn't want to threaten them. Let's go, Rankin.
Take us away.
Rankin scans each face in the crowd, whether they spoke or not.
I'm sorry, friends.
Rankin?
I can't decide your path going forward.
As I said, it was not Hawley who should have been punished; it was me.
I was the one who drove him to madness and violence. It was my decisions that drove him to desperation.
Before this happened, I didn't even realize that not everyone was ready to look death in the eye.
There's no longer any doubt. I don't have what it takes to be a leader. My last and best act as leader is to relinquish this position...
And give you back the power of choice.
Silence.
Long silence.
No one answers. Hardly anyone even breathes, not so much a twitch on their faces.
All who hear the decision are silent, until a trembling voice emerges from the end of the procession.
You're... abandoning... us?
No, Vetochki.
Then, what? What you said... how do we, understand it?
The crowd parts for the one who asked the question.
Vetochki does not move forward. She stands where she is, her fists clenched so hard that her knuckles have turned white.
You say, you won't lead, anymore! After we've come, so far?
Y-You're abandoning, all of us.
I'm not! I must do this, before things get worse for everyone.
No more lives must be lost because of my foolish decisions. We didn't escape from the mines, that hellhole, for this.
H-Hellhole? But it's, my home.
What do you mean?
The mines, mining district, is my... home.
It was, many people's, homes.
Home? I thought you all wanted out, like me.
It's because, of trust. We trust, you—Rankin.
You... you didn't, know?
I thought you chose to leave because you've wanted to for a long time.
You killed, the black bugs. You stood up, and said we, should leave, so... we left.
You caught Hawley, killed him, and said, it was wrong, to leave. You said, it is, a choice.
I-I don't understand! What are we, to you?
Friends, family... it's complicated and hard to define. But it's exactly because you're important to me that—
This is, the home, of the villagers.
Maybe they can, rebuild the village. What about us? Where do... we go?
You took us, so far. If you don't, lead anymore...
How do, we go—go back?
Rankin, tell me...
How do, we live?
Vetochki.
No. The way you, call me. You're treating me, like a child. I-I'm not.
You're not, talking back.
You just proved what I had only guessed.
What?
All I've done up to this point was to force my ideas on you. You didn't understand my plan, you didn't understand the meaning behind my actions.
You trusted me, not your own choices. What will you do when I'm not around to lead you?
I appreciate your trust, but this isn't right.
I'm going to tell you what's happening, and then you can make your choice.
Rankin once again scans each face before him, making sure that he has everyone's undivided attention.
Our ultimate goal is to reach the Kazimierzian border, and find the village of the Infected. In the process, though, this journey must become a reason for Ursus to recognize us.
But it will put us in grave danger, because it challenges the very foundation of the Empire.
People before us have tried and failed, leaving behind nothing but a symbol and a name, on this snowy, swampy tundra.
What if I told you that following me might mean following in their footsteps? It's not something I would recommend for anyone who wants to live, anyone with a brain.
But I never even gave Hawley a choice. That's why he's dead.
He showed me that I must return the choice to you.
The crowd does not answer, but there is some faint commotion.
Slowly, the crowd begins to split. Some stand next to Rankin or behind him, others in front of him.
When do we leave, Rankin?
Once everyone has made their choice.
More and more people move towards Rankin, their footsteps like the delayed ringing of the bell, announcing the end of a shift.
One, two, three... one miscount may mean one more soul lost to the mines.
Who doesn't, want to live?
Why do we, have to choose?
Vetochki can feel them growing more and more distant.
She subconsciously takes a step forward, trying to hold on to something, but someone grabs her and stops her in her tracks.
—Vetochki.
S-Serafina?
Y-You're going too? Where were you? I-I don't know, how to...
Th-They're all leaving! Leaving us to, go die!
It's alright, it's alright. I was just bandaging up Nikto, he cut himself on some broken mirror shards—

It's not important. I heard the last part of your exchange. You want to live. There's nothing wrong with that. You don't have to make yourself walk a path that you don't agree with, and I'm not going with them.
Y-You won't? You're not, leaving?
Yes, me and my team will continue to assist the wounded. Nikto is not leaving either, is he?
...Nikto?
The split crowd continues to move. Raidian could not see Nikto, so she begins scanning for a specific electrical signal with her Arts. It does not take her long.
He's in the rear. He won't leave—
I'm sorry, Gospozha Serafina.
Nikto—
I'm sorry, Vetochki.
I can't go back. You know that I came from Grypherburg. The mines were never my home.
Besides, it's not a sure thing that we'll die. Maybe things won't get so bad with me keeping an eye on the man.
We must make opportunity with our own hands, after all, right?
No. No, no, no!
Why... why?
Why must, it be, like this?
Why must we... always...
...part?
Go home, Vetochki. Don't catch a cold.
Go to bed, and mom and dad will be back when you wake up again.
Be a good girl who doesn't cry or complain, make your babushka happy... and you'll find a piece of chocolate on your pillow when you wake up!
...Strike...
Rights... no... response...
...Execution...
'Mama.'
'Papa.'
'Ditya.'
...'Babushka?'
(Hums)
One word! Just one word!
One word, and my soul will be healed!
(Hums)
—I beg forgiveness... for the sin...
Of killing your child...
'I shall...'

lines.st: 'I shall meditate and share in your pain.'
'For you, for him. May we all repent our sins from the bottom of our hearts.'
'Thy blessings walk, on earth as it is in heaven.'

lines.x: ...Babushka?

lines.st: Baby, baby♪
Close your eyes♪
Someone's outside the window♪
Knocking on the door♪
Baby, baby♪
Hold your breath♪
Someone's putting on a skin♪
Walking in♪
Here it comes♪
Here it comes♪

lines.x: (Hums)
...Babushka...
(Hums)
Babushka... is someone, outside?
No one stops, no one waits.
They're... gone.
They cry, kiss your hand, say they didn't, want to be apart... but they're gone.
Everyone has their own path.
Must this be? But, why...
You know the reason, suffering soul.
Rankin... never taught me... how to, read that watch.
Second hand, clock...? I can hear, the bell.
As always.
Babushka... why is your hand so cold?
The fire is hot.
I'm cold... and it hurts...
But it's not... because of, fire.
I don't understand...
It's alright. Most people live their entire lives in obliviousness.
Until... they die?
Yes, until they die.
(Hums)
Babushka... what are you, singing about?
Love.
But I... hurt?
Because you love.
Love makes people hurt, makes them persevere, makes them docile.
Love makes people strong, makes them wealthy, makes them noble.
You love, and therefore you hurt. I love you, and therefore I share in your pain.
Vetochki says no more. She closes her eyes and curls up tightly, but no longer dares touch the hand that caresses her cheek as usual.
She had heard these words before, back when she did not need to find support for them amongst broken pieces. Now, though, she is no longer sure.
How can one be more wealthy and noble than kings and nobles if one lives in obliviousness?
How can one be stronger than the stick of authority if one is powerless?
The campfire is very near. It should have brought warmth, but Vetochki feels cold in her limbs, her innards, every part of her body—
Passing through the cold, withered hand.
'I shall meditate and share in your pain.'
'May we all...'
'...repent our sins from the bottom of our hearts.'
The voice is simply a repetition, an echo.
The echo points towards... doom.
The tracks end here. Up ahead is...
Hah, I don't think we need to go any further.
What...?
What happened here?

lines.loc: Far North, Ursus

lines.x: This village is the closest to where the miners abandoned their vehicles. I thought we might be able to ask around...
Hard to tell if anyone's still alive. The burn marks on the fence look recent.
We're one step too late... again!
Amiya keeps walking, until the quiet ruins are plain in view.

The fire has long gone out. The bare branches in the surrounding woods cannot even hide the twin moons. The ruins are a sickly black in the pale moonlight, a far cry from what she imagined the village would look like.
Amiya feels a brief daze, then worry, as memories of a similar scene flash back into her mind.
Mantra, we—
(Shakes head)
I'll take a team and search for survivors.
I'll go too.
We'll be fine, and we'll be back soon.
No, let's go together and split up over there.
I think I see someone...
Hello! We're a rescue team! We're here to help!
Do you live in this village? What happened here—
Govno! Govno!
Again!
I did say we were a rescue team, didn't I? Was my Ursine weird?
No, he's just... afraid. I can sense it.
He must live around this place, though, if he showed up here.
Should we follow him?
(Shakes head)
Let's not. We don't want to frighten them.
Who's there?
Oh!
Careful—
I-I'm sorry!
I wasn't, following you, on purpose!
Your clothes, they're like hers, so...
Our clothes? Do you mean this symbol?
(Nods)
What's the name of this woman you're talking about?
S-Serafina...
Raidian's real name.
Is this Serafina a short Liberi with four arms?
You... know her?
Yes! She's our friend, and we've been looking for her.
What's your name? Can you take us to Serafina?
I'm... Vetochki.
Follow me.
A fire, broke out. The others are, outside the village.
Serafina was, helping us. Without her, we couldn't have, gotten here.
You're miners from the Central Mining District?
Yes. You're here, and you say you'll help. Thank goodness...
Now, we can live—
Here we are. Just ahead!
Who's there...?
Dyadya Volya? You still, have a fever... don't sit, at the door!
I'm fine. No fever. The wound on my leg doesn't hurt anymore when I tie it up with clothes.
And who are these people?
They know, Serafina...
We come from the same pharmaceutical company. We've come to help—
Amiya?
Amiya... it's really you!
How did you find this place?
It's really you...
We finally found you!
She runs up to her colleague and gives her a big hug.
Perhaps she should not be showing her emotions in front of others like this, but she cannot decline the opportunity to let her guard down.
Nothing beats the joy of reunion, and the comfort in seeing someone whom you have been worried about for a while.
I wasn't sure when the girl said your name, but now—
I'm so glad.
Is everything alright, Raidian?
I'm fine. Sorry for making you worried.
Team Raidian, all nine members accounted for.
Thank goodness...
How did you find this place? Did someone find the markings I left behind?
A wise course of action.
Mantra! I knew you would be with them.
The Doctor, Mon3tr, and I came when we lost contact with you.
The Doctor is with a researcher that we found, waiting for her to wake up. We'll meet up once communications are restored.
The Doctor is here in Ursus too?
I think we all have a lot of questions to ask each other...
...Rankin left with part of the group after the fire was put out.
The ones here were unwilling or unable to follow, or villagers who lived here to begin with.
I regret not being able to stop the events...
So that's what happened...
Is this Rankin linked to Reunion?
No... as far as I can tell, he never made direct contact with Reunion. He simply believed there was a Reunion position on the Ursus-Kazimierz border.
I've heard fugitive Infected in the Far North talking about it. Does Rhodes Island have any intel?
(Shakes head)
You mean these people have nothing to do with Reunion? They're just flying a banner that they happened to come across?
Who might you be?
Eloysius Gorchekova, Sergeant of the Imperial Guard, Vanguard Battalion. You can call me Eloysius.
What is the Imperial Guard doing here?
Eloysius came to investigate rumors of mutated Infected in the Central Mining District. She helped us get into the district.
It's a pleasure to meet you, Gospozha Raidian.
I am happy for your reunion, but time is short and I'll cut straight to the chase.
Were there any mutated Infected in this group of fugitive miners?
Mutated?
I'm sure you understand what I mean, as an operator of Rhodes Island.
No... at least not the ones who stayed. We did not find any so-called mutated Infected when we examined them.
That's strange... there's certainly something abnormal about these people. If there's nothing out of the ordinary with the ones who stayed, then we must hurry to catch up with the ones who left.
Did they tell you how they planned to proceed to the border? Or any other clues they left behind?
I don't think I'm obliged to report anything to you.
I assure you that my sole purpose here is to discover the truth about the mutated Infected and bring about order.
Deity Grypherburg seeks to protect the interests of the Infected.
I'm afraid I'm not convinced.
At the Tashkent Mines, at Chorna Prison... I never saw a single noble who stood up for the interests of the Infected.
Ah... so you're Ursine too? Third Army, I take it?
That was a long time ago.
Very well... I accept that you have legitimate reasons for your dim opinion of this nation.
Regardless, I am loyal to the motherland, and what you're trying to avoid lines up with what I'm trying to avoid, to an extent.
The miners who left are headed towards the south. They will inevitably pass through Grypherburg on their way to the Kazimierzian border.
Whether they realize it or not, whatever their actual intentions are, they'll be treated as insurgents attempting to attack the capital, once they are discovered.
Only one end awaits them, in that event.
I thought you were going to ask a question, not make a threat.
I'm just stating the truth. I would not have to be so solemn if Gospozha Raidian here would be frank and honest with me. I need to know how these miners left, and why they fled.
Hiding the truth won't do you or them any good.
I see only a group of Infected who are only trying to survive. No one is in any position to denounce them.
You just won't listen, would you?
Gospozha Amiya, we've traveled together for a while now. Could you perhaps help me persuade our strong-minded Liberi here?
There's something even more urgent before that, Eloysius.
The miners and villagers here are in a dangerous situation. They have no supplies and nowhere to go. We can't just abandon them.
We must ensure their safety before talking about the next step.

You don't have to worry about the villagers. I was planning to seek assistance from the Grand Duchess for the rebuilding of their village anyway. Her Imperial Highness has always been generous on such matters.
These miners, though... they're not a problem that a letter will solve.
If you cannot offer assistance to them, then Rhodes Island will.
Very well, I'll help you one more time, for the sake of our cooperation.
We take the miners to Grypherburg.
What?
Yes. We go to Deity Grypherburg and report to His Imperial Majesty the truth about the Infected mutation in the Central Mining District, turning these miners into witnesses.
This will give Grypherburg a reason to protect them, and intercept rather than eliminate the ones who left, until the investigation into the Grand Duchess has concluded, at least.
And after that?
I'll persuade the Chairman to request that His Imperial Majesty grant amnesty to these Infected, then ask Rhodes Island to intervene on the grounds of medical assistance.
Or maybe you only need these miners for now, and you'll have no further use of them after you bring the Grand Duchess down.
Otherwise, you have no reason to spare these Infected. I know Ursus. Whatever warmth the Tsar wants to bring to this cold soil... the Infected will never be part of it.
Take that back.
I'm just stating the truth.
How dare you—
—Maybe that's true for Ursus, but I've seen enough of Eloysius that I'm willing to trust her.
Amiya?
Sorry, Eloysius. Raidian's simply expressing her concerns. I'm sure we all know how Ursus has historically approached the Infected problem.
(Nods)
Very well... thank you for your trust, even if it's only towards me.
The Guardswoman bows at them exaggeratedly, and casts one last provoking look at Raidian.
...I have one final question.
Are you authorized to do this, Sergeant?
Maybe you've been away from Grypherburg too long to know the weight that the name Gorchekova carries there.
On the name of my house—
You don't need to worry about anything else, other than providing medical assistance.
...You're telling the truth.
Of course I am.
Even if the plan works, we must get the miners to agree first.
Yes. I'll go.
I'll explain the situation to them and ask what they think.
There you are, Vetochki...
There's something that we need to tell everyone. We have a choice: leave here and go to Deity Grypherburg together—
Grypherburg?
Mama said, black locusts, and cherry trees, grow on the streets, of Grypherburg.
The sky, is bright, the fowlbeasts, build nests, they don't, fly away.
...There's spring there. That, Grypherburg?
Yes, that Grypherburg. Do you want to go?

Yes! I wanna—
*cough*!
She is so eager that she lets cold air get into her throat, causing pain from the dryness.
I know you do. But what about the others?
I've never, left the mines. I...
We have to ask the others whether they're like you, if they're willing to trust us and go to Grypherburg.
I-I'll ask. I know, they will!
Before you... came back, Darya said, we can't, get away.
Even if we, wanted to, go back, people outside would, stop us. They'll... burn us, to death.
I'll tell her, it's not, like that.
You're right. It's not like that.
Darya! Darya?
Darya! We'll go, to Deity Grypherburg! Serafina has, a way!
We'll all, go!
...Darya?
The girl is sitting straight with her back against the wall. Her eyes are closed, exhaustion upon her young face like shadows.
She still breaths, but she is not doing any more than the minimum required to live.
No... we can't... stay here...
Come with me—hang on, I'll carry, you!
Darya...!
Probably just tired. Let her rest, we'll find others—
Vetochki looks around, hoping that her friend's silence is just a coincidence.
Dyadya Volya...
She walks over and shakes them one by one, only to find that their backs are all straight and stiff, just like Darya.
Some are kneeling while others are standing, but they are all facing the old woman with bowed heads inside the house, as though they can hear nothing else.
Vetochki looks up, and a word slips from her mouth.
...Babushka?
Raidian follows Vetochki's fearful gaze.
An ancient voice fills the space in the direction that they are all looking.
Welcome...
The one who Ursus remembers.
The figure's face and eyes are concealed by a hood, but somehow, Raidian has a feeling that the figure is not looking at her.
What did you say?
Sorry for the interruption, but you're taking too long.
Everyone! I have a proposal that can ensure your safety, to the greatest extent possible.
Sergeant—
Leave the Far North with us and come to Deity Grypherburg.
The State Council will protect you, as long as you are willing to testify against the Grand Duchess.
His Imperial Majesty must know what happened here. Ursus must know what happened here!
Once everything is settled, Rhodes Island, where this lady you all know came from, will provide further treatment.
Now, anyone willing to come with me—
They're not listening.
The electrical signals coming from them...

lines.st: 'Meditate.'

lines.x: Suddenly, a strange coldness penetrates Raidian's cerebral cortex, virtually nailing her to the ground.
She is unwilling to offer any further warnings, even if she was just about to do so just a moment ago.
What...?
Even the most chaotic electrical signal should have been benign to her, but now she turns it into a knife to keep herself lucid.
Raidian shuts out her sensations with Originium Arts, attempting to break the shapeless restraint.
What are you—
Argh—
We must—go!
Take—everyone—leave!

lines.st: 'Mantra... is this the name you go by now?'

lines.x: Mantra, I sense cold and... pain?
A vortex, inviting me to step inside...
Amiya, do not look! Do not listen!
I—I—
Ahh!
Shut out your senses with Arts, Amiya.
Same for you, Elysium. Stay here while I find out what's happening.
Mantra!
Stay back! This is not normal. They can't hear us speak—
Whatever it is...
It's calling to me.
And I must face it.
Mantra closes her eyes, and begins to picture the closed space in her mind like a mental blueprint.
She steps into the house. She will find and anchor each active consciousness here, awaken them and make them 'hear'.
At least, that is what should have happened.
No...
There is only...
Silence?
Red.
Piercing red.
Red, like the blood on the snow, encroaching upon the retina.
Then, she opens her eyes.

lines.st: (Hums)
What makes us equal?

lines.x: Perseverance.
Faith.
Pain.

lines.st: No, no, no. It is love.
What needs to be saved?

lines.x: The soul?
The soul.
The soul!

lines.st: Yes, yes, yes. The soul.
It is not our body that needs to be saved, but our souls.
Love and the soul are one and whole. Love makes us endure, makes us believe, makes us persevere.
And we shall meditate and share in the pain of others.
Make love pure, save the soul.
For you, for him, for all who endure pain...
Desire not your own life, decide not your own death.

lines.x: I endure.
I believe!
I persevere...
Gosudar-batyushka—

lines.st: 'Gosudar-Yurodstvo!'

lines.x: Ahead is the tunnel where we first found Oka.
It should have been placed under lockdown soon after... but now, it looks completely abandoned.
The good news is that Originium particle activity has also dropped after work stopped. Infection risk should be minimal as long as you do not touch the deactivated Originium crystals.
I'm not detecting any other lifeforms. We should be able to get in without a fight.

opts: We still don't know what happened here.
The Patrol Units and guards could return any time.
Time is of the essence.
Let's do this quickly.

lines.x: Are you sure this is the right way, Ksenia?
I don't see anything.
Sorry. I've only been down here once before...
Let me find the emergency power switch.
Here it is.
Ok, looks like this is the right way. All we need to do is to go in the opposite direction from the mine carts.
Opposite direction? I thought we're going deeper.
It leads to somewhere other than the mine tunnels. I don't know what it's actually for, but the carts only carry goods, not people.
What about the cart? Can we use it?
There are no structural issues, but it's been abandoned for so long that there's only enough emergency power for the lights. I don't think it's a good idea.

opts: I agree.
We can walk.

lines.x: Mm.
You two, get on the cart.
What?

opts: There's no safety belt!
We can walk!

lines.x: Get on, Doctor. We can save time that way.
But it won't start—
I'll take care of it.
Do you need me to lift you up, Doctor?

opts: ...I can do it myself!

lines.x: Are you ready?
What are you planning—
—Whoa?!

opts: We'll be thrown off the cart.
The cart's going to fall apart.

lines.x: It'll be fine. Just sit tight.
Here we go!
Thank goodness the tunnels look intact.
But we should have started a fire before coming down... it's a lot wetter than the outside. The difference is almost as great as the Iberian coast compared to inland Ursus.
Of course, there is a dramatic difference in temperature as well... are you alright?
I'm fine. What about you, Doctor?

opts: Every part of my brain feels equally shaken.
You looked like you enjoyed it, Mon3tr.

lines.x: I kept the speed steady.
Mon3tr is a very good cart driver. Or cart pusher.
Sounds like the problem's not on my end.

opts: I have a question, Ksenia.
When was Oka first discovered in the mines?

lines.x: I'm not sure exactly when. The first sample arrived at the lab a month ago.
That was when I learned of the appearance of this heretofore unheard-of substance in the Originium mines.

opts: But it's strange...
This substance that appeared in the mines...
What made the lab decide to perform human experiments?

lines.x: Infected exposed to Oka in this tunnel were subject to unusual mutations.
Their infection readings soared, but their vital signs remained stable, 'healthy' even...
That sounds familiar.
Have you encountered other patients like this? Have they come into contact with Oka?

opts: There may be another possibility.
We're not sure yet...

lines.x: In any case... the Ursine government wanted to replicate the 'miracle'.
I suppose they wanted a docile labor force that did not fear illness or pain... perhaps even employ them in combat.

opts: I'm not surprised the government would react that way...
They haven't realized the potential danger.

lines.x: I think we're getting close.
This is as far as the rail goes. We'll have to proceed on foot.
The three of you move forward in the darkness.
You hear before you see. It is the heavy sound of water.
The three of you look up and slowly open your eyes.
You soon realize that the light in the tunnel is not coming from outside. It is coming from the stationary liquid in front of you on the ground—and the 'waterfall' at the very end.
It glitters like obsidian, refracting light from a crack high above as it falls, making the cave bright as early morning after a shower.

opts: ...An obsequent river.

lines.x: You can tell?
Yes. Like an obsequent river, the Oka cannot flow outwards along the dip, so it can only flow backwards, until it lies 'still' like this.
But it continues to flow in a way, even without external forces acting on it. That's why they named it 'Oka'—river.
The liquid is everywhere.
It's like a natural karst cave. You can't even tell that it used to be a mine.
Even stranger, Oka itself is almost like water.

opts: What do you mean?
How could it be?

lines.x: Well... in a manner of speaking.
Oka becomes highly stable after it is allowed to stand still for a while. It can even be used as a universal solvent.
Wait! We still shouldn't touch it with bare hands—
Oh...
Mon3tr bends over and collects some liquid with a test tube. She is not wearing gloves, or any other form of protection.
Her expression grows solemn as she shakes the liquid in the test tube with wet hands.

opts: Mon3tr?

lines.x: You should not touch it directly—I have protective equipment. You can tell me if you want a sample, and I'll collect it!
(Whisper) Doctor... I know what this is.
What?

opts: Ksenia, the samples...
Can you get some samples from around the waterfall?

lines.x: Oh, okay.
Wait here. I'll be back soon.
...Be careful.
This liquid that they call Oka... it's Originium.

opts: Originium...
Why does it look that way?

lines.x: It's complicated.
Do you remember the 'Transmitter' that Rhine Lab was studying, or the 'Corazonix' that is often mentioned in Iberian alchemy?

opts: I do.
Are they related?

lines.x: This is their prototype... or perhaps I should say, the original form.

opts: What the Transmitter and Corazonix have in common...
They both serve as a kind of medium.

lines.x: Yes. A medium that reacts directly with the human body, or to store information.

opts: So that's why they are both liquid?

lines.x: I think so...
You understand, Doctor, that Originium is both a vehicle for information, and a vehicle for energy.
Both the Transmitter and Corazonix re-record Originium energy through specific means, in order to record different types of information.
The Oka here is the remnant of Originium, after its energy was extracted in an even more effective manner.
So it's really a form of 'universal solvent', and this explains why the Infected who came into contact with Oka became 'mutated'.
Because of its nature as drained Originium, Oka neutralizes the Originium activity inside the Infected, while creating the perfect conditions for the continued growth of Originium.
It allowed the Infected to retain their vital functions temporarily, but does not change the fact that death would inevitably follow soon.
Meanwhile, all the uninfected individuals injected with Oka died of severe rejection, due to the lack of active Originium in their bodies...

opts: Its presence here in such quantities...
It means...

lines.x: This is what we feared the most...
The Kolossal Prime Vein is drying up.
The rumors we heard, that Ursus is accelerating the extraction of Originium... it's all to hide the exhaustion of the ore vein.
It began here in the northmost parts of Ursus, and it will only accelerate.
Within four months, the Originium deposits in Ursus will drop to thirty percent of previous levels.

opts: This is the real reason why the Grand Duchess put the mining district on lockdown.
They can't hide it forever.

lines.x: You did not need to ask the question. One gaze exchanged with Mon3tr, and both of you have the same name in mind.
The only one who could have done this—

opts: Priestess.

lines.x: There's no one else.

opts: Why would she do this?

lines.x: The exhaustion of the ore vein cannot be stopped or reversed.
Ursus will collapse.

opts: Not just Ursus.
Exhaustion on such a scale will have ramifications across Terra.
Is this what she's after?

lines.x: I have an idea!
Oka is also a relic of the old civilization... a by-product of your experiments.
If she caused the exhaustion of the ore vein, then there might be some useful information left within.
See if you can read it.

opts: How would I...?

lines.x: Our means are limited. You'll have to insert your hand, like I did when I collected the sample.
Don't worry, it won't harm you.
Following Mon3tr's instructions, you crouch down and immerse your hands into the liquid Oka.
You wait.

opts: You do not feel any different.

lines.x: You await some kind of change, or response, like the 'miracles' that happened so many times before.
Nothing happened.
The product of a previous civilization seems to reject you, denying your connection to it.

opts: Could it be that it has already lost its ability to hold information?
Or perhaps, she had no intention of leaving any information to us.

lines.x: It's possible.
Mon3tr! Doctor!
Come! I found something!
There's a hole behind the waterfall. When I climbed up and stood there—
I heard a sound of an unusual frequency from the cave. It didn't seem natural—no, it definitely isn't natural!
It's like... it's trying to communicate some sort of information through audio waves!
Huh?

opts: Let's check it out.

lines.x: This is—
This space was hidden behind the waterfall?

opts: It's... impressive.

lines.x: Listen carefully to the sound of the liquid impacting the rocks! Some of the sounds are of a particular frequency, like it's trying to draw attention to itself. It's very different, and you can definitely tell.
I tried to decipher the ones I heard. It took a while to record the various pitches—
There! Did you hear that?
All I hear is the rain! I can't even hear you speak—
Doctor?
You close your eyes and allow yourself to be immersed in sound.
Until you identify the sound that is constantly changing in frequency.

opts: I hear it.

lines.x: —Did you say you hear it—
The sound rushes into your consciousness. Sometimes it is formed from several high frequencies, sometimes it is short and sharp, like a meticulous music score.
You soon realize that it represents some sort of code, or perhaps language.
You try to recall all the languages that you know, categorizing them in your mind, and you soon realize that you are hearing the same word, just in different languages.
I've copied it down...
It's just a hunch, but if we decipher it using the standard encoding for communication between nomadic cities...
I think it's referring to the same word, in many different languages. I could identify Ursine and Gaulish from it! I think they both mean—

lines.st: 'Unknown language'
'спорить'
'dibattito'
'débat'
'biànlùn'

lines.x: Upon the rocks, the liquid in the mine is communicating the same word at you—

lines.st: 'Debate.'
'Debate.'

lines.x: Yes, Dr. Doctor.
If you still believe you made the right choice.
If you still believe in the future you described.
Then refute me with reality, and prove to me...
That thing which you hold so firm...

lines.st: 'Hope.'

opts: ...It's an invitation.

lines.x: What?

opts: I said, it's an invitation.
One that I must accept.

lines.x: (Whisper) You think it's a message from Priestess?

opts: Yes.
I know what she means...
We should leave, quickly.

lines.x: We'll go back to the lab once Ksenia has gathered her samples.
We must try to contact Amiya...

opts: I wonder how Amiya's team is doing.
I hope Amiya has made contact with Raidian.
Did you hear a sound?

lines.x: What sound?

opts: Something heavy stepping in water.

lines.x: The waterfall is pretty loud.
Are you sure it's not just your ears ringing? Perhaps lingering effects from having to process too much information just now...
Wait.
Ksenia? Is that you?

opts: It's not Ksenia's footsteps. Not the right weight or rhythm.

lines.x: What's... that—
Frenzied crying, frenetic breathing, thick mucous liquid dripping from the fangs.
Its entire body is covered in a hard layer of dark-red Originium, forcing it to stand on its two hind legs.
You see it raise its front paws—
Not paws. A pair of withered hands, indistinguishable from that of a human.

opts: An Originium-infected manglerbeast.

lines.x: I have the samples!
Why are you just standing there?
The strange manglerbeast, which seems so removed from reality, is right behind her.

opts: Run, Ksenia!

lines.x: Run, Ksenia! Don't look back!
I'll get her. Go to the mine cart, Doctor, and we'll meet you there!
Go!
What—
The researcher cannot help but look back as she starts running.
She hardly has the time to swallow her scream.
Gosudar-batyushka—
What is that thing?!

opts: Has it...?
Stopped moving?

lines.x: I think so...
The roaring and the heavy breathing have ceased. Did we kill it?
But it's still standing—
Don't move! I'll check.
Mon3tr ascertains that the other two are at a safe distance, and approaches it on her own.

The manglerbeast stands quietly, no sign of the madness that had engulfed it only moments ago, its strange forelimbs hanging still in front of its body. Part of the Originium covering it has been chipped away.
Mon3tr approaches carefully.
She observes the manglerbeast. It was dangerous to begin with, and its mutations make it look strange, almost otherworldly.
The influence of Originium is turning it into a 'human' physiologically...
It must be in a great deal of pain...

opts: Mon3tr, look out!
It's moving again!

lines.x: Oh—
But the manglerbeast does not attack or even move from its spot. It falls toward the ground—
Until its forelimbs touch the ground again with a thud.

opts: Now's our chance to leave, Mon3tr.
We should leave and go back to the lab, now.

lines.x: No, Doctor.

opts: Mon3tr?

lines.x: It's still breathing!
And it sounds like it's... in pain? I think.
I can't sense another's emotions like Amiya can, and this is just a mutated animal. But...
I know it will die, even if we don't do anything.
The Originium has impaled its throat. Every part of its body is in pain. It would claw out its own mutated organs if it could.
Perhaps this is why it attacked us. It's not in control of itself anymore.

opts: Let's end its pain, then.

lines.x: Yes.
I can do it. I can...
End its pain.
She walks towards the fallen beast. Standing next to it, she puts her claws against a piece of fur with fewer crystals.
Mon3tr gazes at the manglerbeast with incredible calm in her eyes.
Then, she surgically extinguishes the mutated manglerbeast's last, intermittent breathing.
Let's go, Doctor.
Back to the lab.
Mantra continues to listen.
She knows she cannot trust what she sees, whether it is twisted by intention, or reflecting reality by accident.
And so she listens, until the first word of prayer drops in the crowd like the first snow.
Yurodstvo...
'Gabrielus.'

lines.st: I'm listening.

lines.x: Then stop.
Stop—
Stop it all!

lines.st: I cannot stop what never began.
What you see is only in your mind.

lines.x: Mantra stops trying to communicate. She steps forward and tries to steady the trembling red, white and black threads.
As she stretches out her hand—
—Mantra—
Mantra!
What?
You were completely motionless.
I've never seen you like this... what happened?
Did you hear that?
Hear what?
The old woman does not immediately answer.
She looks around and realizes that the remaining miners look different from before. She frowns and glances around herself.
Where is Eloysius?
She heard me and ran outside.
So you did tell us to leave.
Yes, but I don't remember why... this is strange.
Mental Arts.
Are you sure? I've received specific training. I would not be defenseless against such attacks.
Check for short-term memory interference, Raidian.
What sort of interference?
Interference that I can create.
One time, I had Mechanist forget that he wanted to ask you about borrowing Dendie and Axie. Not by wiping the memory, but by blocking its formation.
I remember... that was the first time you tried to use your Arts like that, I think? We forget when information in our brain is not successfully stored in the memory.
Yes. It was your idea. You are far more experienced than I am in the understanding and utilization of human bioelectrical signals.
You think someone here uses the same sort of Arts?
Unsure. Perhaps I just saw and heard more than you did.
What do you mean?
The Yurodstvo of Ursus may be here, Raidian.
Yurodstvo? Why do you bring that up—
The Yurodstvo is here? But the last Yurodstvo was burned alive in Grypherburg two years ago. Could another be born so soon?
I don't know. I'm going to find out.
If it's really the Yurodstvo, and it was hidden in the group, without me detecting it... then it's extremely dangerous.
I'll put the others on alert. Be careful.
The danger is not physical. Not yet, at least.
I'll seek help if necessary.
Don't forget it.
Mantra turns around and walks towards the place where the red was, a few moments ago.
The assembled miners either give way to her, or reach out to her.
Who are you... looking for?
We're still going, to Grypherburg, right?
We'll l-live...
(Nods)
But they look like, they don't want, to leave? I asked Dyadya Volya. He doesn't want, to leave.
He'd rather, his wounds rot, than to run.
But isn't surviving, the most important thing?
You're not, talking...
Stranger.
Are you going to see Nadia? Then go on your knees, and raise your eyes to her.
See her, repent, and be forgiven. Then you will realize how foolish you were, for struggling and failing to find peace.
Mantra neither assents nor dissents. She does not speak to them with Arts. She simply nods and passes them by, staring straight into the deepest reaches of the room.
You will understand... when you are in her presence.
A hunched, tiny grey-white figure sits at the end of the room like a statue.
—Yurodstvo?!
Impossible. It's impossible! The Yurodstvo is dead!
The Tsar granted the madman the mercy of defending himself before his sentence was pronounced.
Seven days! He engaged in sophistry for seven days without stopping, without eating or drinking.
He had the audacity to stand in the middle of the State Council, telling everyone who came to listen—
'I forgive you.'
His Imperial Majesty finally had enough and sentenced him to death, personally, but brainwashed believers attempted to raid the execution, resulting in the biggest riot in Grypherburg in a decade.
We know all that.
If Mantra said it was the Yurodstvo, she must have had the evidence to make the deduction.
All we need to do is protect ourselves and wait to hear from her.
Mantra does not kneel as the miners instructed.
She steps into the shadow, facing forward, as though there are a pair of eyes looking backwards in front of her.
I know you can 'hear'.

lines.st: I hear your questions.

lines.x: I hope you're more than just a pale imitation of my Arts and my way of speaking, Yurodstvo.
'Yurodstvo'... if that is the name you need me to have.
Explain.
Gabrielus, Solomida, Bessmert, Aurora, Nadia... or, perhaps, Yurodstvo.
I am all of them.
Why did you hide amongst these suffering people?
Because it is a journey of proselytization.
I shall walk this vast land and redeem the people from their sufferings.
And because I saw and heard her.
I stayed because of her pain.
Who is she?
A suffering soul.
You deceived her and made her call you 'Nadia'?
I never deceived.
You lie.
She asked as you did. I answered as I do.
That was all.
A meaningless response. What are you?
You saw me.
I see shadow without order.
Yes, I am.
But not in the eyes of others.
Explain.
People see in me the reflection of their perception and expectation.
I thought, of all the souls, you alone would understand why I exist so.
After all...
You are the unfinished sculpture. You were once to become—
'Me.'
Mantra keeps her eyes fixed on the strange apparition in front of her, but she is unsure whether she can identify any useful information from it.
A long time ago, you cut away a black piece of rotten flesh.
So did I. It pursued me, and I repelled it, like cutting off a piece of rotten flesh between breaths.
An absurd analogy.
No, a simple principle.
If it is rotten and no longer connected to the rest of your body...
Why keep it?
This is...
Your memory?
But it is mine—
No. The Royal Guard... if you can influence a Royal Guard, you can influence the miners.
You killed it. You became me.
If this is the only thing we share in common...
The body, and rotten flesh. Your relationship to the Royal Guard—the Yurodstvo was the source of the Royal Guard experiments.
No sound comes from the trembling shadows, neither affirmation nor denial.
Mantra begins to back off and leaves the shadow's edge.
...I need support, Raidian.
I'm here.
The conjecture is confirmed.
Also, additional information...
The Yurodstvo hid amongst the miners and helped them kill the Royal Guard.
...I see.
What do you need us to do?
I need you to continue to contact other Rhodes Island assets in Ursus, and ask Amiya to delay Eloysius.
We can't leave yet, at least not until I find a way to rouse the miners.
Have Elysium standby inside the range of my Arts. I may need his assistance.
What's your plan?
Remove the influence that the Yurodstvo has on the miners.
You think the Yurodstvo is controlling the miners and causing them to refuse to leave?
Yes.
We cannot kill the Yurodstvo directly, but it can kill anyone here, anytime it chooses to.
Maybe Amiya can help.
Maybe. But Amiya does not understand the Yurodstvo, and I do not want to her to become involved without any understanding.
The Yurodstvo's powers are similar to mine. This is why I am the only one who can make this decision.
It relies on the cognition of others to establish itself. I must find the key to its establishment of the 'Nadia' identity, in order to remove its influence.
You have someone you suspect.
...Yes.
Mantra casts her gaze at the surrounding miners, and eventually upon the girl who walks past her to the Yurodstvo.
She mutters in a daze, in bewilderment and sorrow.
I just need time to confirm.
Cold night, long night.
The bare earth is covered in frost. Upon it are haphazard footsteps, stretching into the distance where the sun fades beneath the horizon.
Scattered black dots are moving slow, at the place where the earth meets the night sky.
Lake Neva... is frozen.
This year is bloody cold. It's not even winter yet... what are you laughing about?
I remember that in the winter of 31, Igor Velikiy defeated the army defending Grypherburg on Lake Neva, spelling the end of the Hippogryph Kingdom.
And what about it? You want to lead us in an assault on Deity Grypherburg?
No. Not yet, at least.
Hmph... it's another story that I'm thinking of.
Around the year 150, a famous playwright wrote a play about Igor Velikiy before he entered Grypherburg.
His intention was to celebrate the friendship between Igor and the Hippogryph noble Mikhail, who were comrades in the revolution overthrowing the Hippogryph Kingdom.
But it was lambasted throughout Ursus because it mentioned a 'compromise' between Igor and certain Hippogryph nobles, whereby Igor gave up some power in exchange for their support.
The Ursus refused to accept it. They considered it a slander against Igor Velikiy.
The playwright paid the price for a story that he claimed to be true to history.
It was only many years after his death, when the original agreement between Igor and the Hippogryph nobles was discovered, that people learned he had been telling the truth.
A fine story.
Are you trying to tell me that the legend that I worship is just a figment of human imagination?
I'm trying to tell you that even Igor knew the importance of flexibility.
We should stop here, Rankin.
Grypherburg is in the way, and its defenses are on a completely different level from the Far North.
What if they find us? It won't be the Patrol Units waiting for us, but the Armies of Ursus.
Winter will find us in the wilderness if we continue to take detours and fail to reach the border in time.
I would much rather deal with a patrol or two, than Ursine winter.
Do we really have to go to the Kazimierzian border, though? Find your legendary Reunion?
You know how far it is, never mind the enemies that we might encounter on the way. Look at our people, our food, even our clothes. Do we have enough to make it?
Let's say we actually make it to the border. What if it's not an Infected village that awaits us there, but another mine? What do we do then?
What other way is there? Where can we go?
There must be a way...
I know! We can go east! Nikolsk. It's an old city, almost abandoned now, and there are even fewer people in the surrounding villages.
We could go there, split up and hide in the villages... that's the only way for us to survive.
And live like slaves for the rest of our lives?
I gave you a choice, Nikto. I gave everyone a choice.
I thought everyone chose to come with me to live like men.
But you shouldn't wager everyone's lives on a castle in the sky—
I took a good look, Rankin.
The outpost ahead is manned. The searchlights are on... it hasn't been abandoned.
If we can't get through this way, do we have to...?
You hear that, Rankin?
Searchlights... how many searchlights did you see?
I... I don't really remember. Two... maybe three?
Think! How many exactly?
Two! Yes, I remember now. There were only lights on the east and west sides. It's two lights!
Two lights... so a garrison of a hundred or so. Maybe...
Rankin!
Look behind you, Rankin... this is all the people we have...
This is not a revolutionary army. They just want to live.
Are you going to send them into the mines from which there is no return, like the mine owners did?
Rankin looks at the crowd behind him and sees their expressions under the lamplight.
Pain, fatigue, helplessness, fear...
Perhaps Nikto is right. Their clothes are too thin and they cannot take it. But he feels no cold, for some reason.
No...
I won't throw away any lives. Even if sacrifice may be inevitable, I won't allow anyone to die without meaning.
We can't go back to a life of slavery...
Follow me. There's another way.
There's such a long mine tunnel underground?
What's going on, Rankin? How did you know where the mine tunnel was here?
Someone had an idea a few years back.
There are more than a thousand mines in the Far North, each with hundreds of workers, yet they are all disconnected from each other. What if we changed that?

We could connect the tunnels—no one knows the underground better than we do. We could weave a web beneath the frozen soil and link everyone, sharing information and helping each other in the hour of need.
Have you been preparing all these years? Were you planning for this day?
I'm no prophet. I simply began with a very basic idea—we should unite and help each other.
At the beginning, my influence extended only to a dozen mines in the area. I was powerless beyond that.
Later, I heard that other people were doing the same things in other places... it's a pleasant surprise to find that this tunnel looks relatively safe.
Where does it lead?
The Volgrad Mining District, southeast of Grypherburg, in the southernmost part of Ursus. If we can reach it through the tunnels, we'll be able to circumvent the capital's defenses.
But you better pray there's no army on our tail, or it'll be an easy opportunity to wipe us out all at once.
No such thing as a path without risk.
Wait... I smell alcohol.
Impossible. How could there be—
Get down!
A flash of cold light lunges out from the darkness, breaking the only lit lamp.
What the—
Get down! Don't move, and don't fire back!
Blyat! They found us!
Call for help!
Wait! Stop! We're friends!
Rankin stands and shouts at the top of his lungs, his voice echoing in the deepest reaches of the cavern.
You...
You're not the Patrol Units?
That's what we were about to ask... I suppose the answer is no.
Thank goodness! I thought they found our position.
Where did you come from?
The Central Mining District.
Central? I didn't know there were any survivors there!
We heard the guards started a massacre. We thought there wasn't anybody left alive.
I can see why there would be such rumors...
But they're not true. We're still alive. We defeated the Patrol Units and escaped. We haven't given up on our struggle.
That's the best news I've heard in a while!
One moment.
We're trying to get to the Volgrad Mining District, in the south, through these tunnels. Can we—
The south? It's no good. The tunnel leading to the south has collapsed. That's why we've been stuck here.
I see...
Again, Rankin! Again! Why is every path a dead end?
Wait... Rankin? THE Rankin?
My name's Rankin. I don't know anything about 'The' Rankin.
It's really you! Rankin from Central! You're real—you're alive!
I see my name has taken flight on its own...
We can talk about that later! Let me take you to the living area.
You folks look like you're in real bad shape. We have some food and water, not a lot, but we can spare some.
Living area? Are there a lot of you living here?
Yes...
A lot.
They follow the lone light of the lamp and walk into the tunnel. Gradually, they begin to see the light at the end, and hear the faint sound of talking.
They go around a corner to see a large number of people—there must be hundreds of them—living in a tiny space.
Some are wearing miner's outfits, others simple cotton clothes. There are elderly, and there are children... with simple living tools around them.
This is where they live.
You...
What's going on? Didn't you guys go on patrol? Why did you bring so many people back? Who are they?
Friends! Let me introduce this man here—Rankin!
Rankin! He's Rankin!
I've heard the name before! You were the one who had the idea of linking all the mining districts!
You're not as tall as the stories say, but I guess you're tall enough...
Did you fight the Kazimierzians? Do the Firewatch really exist?

They rush forward to see the figure of legend. There is reverence, there is joy, and there is a little bit of disappointment in seeing the real man behind the myth, but all culminate in an excited call for his name.
Rankin, Rankin.
Alright, that's enough. Our friends have run into some trouble and they need rest.
Get some bread from the storage room, Zoya. The big loaves.
...Why are you living here?
That's a long story...
The Neva Dam burst three years ago, flooding the nearby mines. The overseer refused to let us come out from underground, fearing that we might flee to a nearby city.
The mine eventually collapsed, splitting it in half. Half of us were drowned. We were the lucky half.
We thought all we could do was wait for death, until we found a passage below that led to another mine.
We got some food from there, but we didn't dare go above ground, so we stayed underground...
And you've been living here? How?
We helped deliver cargo for other mines. Some kind-hearted people shared food with us. Sometimes we stole from the Patrol Units... just like musbeasts, eh.
We continued to expand the passages, to the Ural in the east and Kras in the west, linking up a lot of mines and districts.
We've taken in refugees who fled the other mines. We've got maybe a thousand people who are somewhat fit to move.
There are children here...
Yes... she's two years-old, and she's never seen the sun...
Blyat!
No matter what, we're still alive! All thanks to you, Rankin!
We'd have been long dead if you didn't come up with your idea!
No... I did nothing. Don't thank me. You found your path of life on your own.
But it shouldn't have been like this. This shouldn't have been your life.
But... what could we do, Rankin?
Of course we don't want to live in this hole forever, but where else can we go?
...Come with me.
Where to?
South.
To the Kazimierzian border. We'll find the village that belongs to the Infected, land that we can live on.
We'll go together.
Calm down, Rankin.
Did you forget about the Lake Neva Outpost? Did you think you could sneak past them with so many people?
Then we'll take it by force.
You're out of your mind—
I know what I'm talking about, Nikto!
I'm confident.
The Lake Neva Outpost will have at most a hundred men, and no heavy weapons.
Three hundred. Give me three hundred pairs of hands that can hold a crossbow and a hoe, and we'll take the Lake Neva Outpost before they have a chance to send a distress signal.
By the time the regular supply delivery arrives weeks later, we'll be long gone, on the vast Barton Plains!
We'll only be one step away from the goal. Nothing can stop us.
No... you've gone mad.
I don't understand... why do you always force yourself into the riskiest option? Why do you—
Rankin ignores his objections and extends his hand to the crowd.
I see your vision, Rankin, and I'm willing to go with you to take the outpost.
But I can't make the others follow you. They'll have to decide their own fates.
The miner shakes Rankin's hand and goes to stand behind him.
All who are willing to go with Rankin to take the outpost, come forward.
Feet shuffle in the crowd, and several youths take their place behind Rankin.
Others remain hesitant, or find their own family hanging onto their shirts.
Whatever the others say, I'm with you, Rankin.
I don't know what that place for the Infected on the Kazimierzian border is like, but it can't be worse than here.
We trust you because you're Rankin. You're the hero that united us. No matter what end awaits us—
Rankin halts those words by raising a hand. He looks down, takes a deep breath, then looks up again at the crowd, his eyes resolute.
Have you ever seen spring in Ursus?
Silence.
I have, when I was a soldier with the border guard.
When the snow melts, green grass grows from the soil again, and the migratory fowlbeasts come back.
Sometimes, the sun is out all day, so bright it almost drives you mad, and you can only sleep by pulling your blanket over your head.
The sun, the grass, the fowlbeasts, the wind... none of those exist in the mines.
But it's not because we don't deserve it!
It's not too much to ask. These are things that you and I, your children, all of us, deserve as human beings!
Rankin's words echo in the dark mine, and in the hearts of all who are listening.
It will be dangerous. I won't lie to you, nor will I force anyone to join us.
But sacrifices are a part of any struggle. There will always be those who fall by the wayside, or leave of their own accord.
Whether they fell or left on their own, whether they were brave or timid, remember those who are around you, and remember that you once stood together and shared the same pain.
We will always be comrades.
Some may fall tonight, and some may live. Those who live will pave the path to tomorrow for the rest.
We shall raise a torch upon the outpost to remember the fallen, that survivors may remember, and the oppressor will see.
Before we leave, I want to tell you how I feel.
I hope you will join me and contribute your strength.
Only by taking this step can we go back to the surface, bathe in the sunlight, and become human again!
Rankin's chest rises and falls. He feels hot blood flow through his veins, and his strength return to him, and he raises his arms as he speaks.
More and more people come over to him, as they did when he first made his rallying cry in the mine shafts of the Central Mining District.
Brothers, sisters, remember the inhuman lives that we once led.
We were herded into the mines like beckbeasts, using our lives to dig for Originium to warm the bed of others. This is not the law; it is murder.
Yes, murder. They tried to shut our mouths, keep us captive until we die, stop us from thinking and make us numb, until we are no different from animals.
But we did not forget how to struggle. Deep underground, we connected the mines, and broke through our prison of death.
But that's not enough. We can't just keep running away. We must face our foe head-on.
Someone might ask, 'What do we do, Rankin?'
It's difficult, but it is also easy. The first thing to do is take the first step.
Tonight's battle will be the first step. More people will learn who we are, and all those who are oppressed like we are will know that they're not alone!
Do not fear. There's no need to fear. Those who are high above are not strong. We are strong!
We have nothing to lose except our lives... if they won't give us our right, then we'll fight for it with our lives!
Ask me. Ask me, 'What do we do?' 'What will become of us?'
History has shown us time and again that it doesn't forgive oppressors. If punishment has not yet come, it's only because it still needs time, and anger that will set everything on fire.
Igor Velikiy ripped the wings off the high-and-mighty Hippogryphs. If his descendants are doing the same things the Hippogryphs once did, then we will drag them down from their thrones!
Our weapons will once again force Deity Grypherburg's golden gates open. Our pickaxes will dig a hole in this land, and tear its ugly, greedy face to pieces!
All of Ursus will see us, fear us, recognize us.
Then, we will have a bright future.
You know...
I rather like Bresk this time of year.
The sun no longer rises and falls, there is no distinction between day and night... and there is no need to pretend that anything is under the light anymore.
The Marshal calls it 'The Autumn of Troubles'.
Time is of the essence, Your Imperial Highness. The situation grows more tense as we speak, as I enjoy your hospitality and your tea.
The Marshal awaits your reply.
I see even Marshal Evgeny is flustered. It seems there is truly great trouble in Ursus.
You are correct.
The mellow response from Deity Grypherburg is unacceptable—The Marshal is not the only one who thinks so.
I expect he has realized how wisely his father acted in that insurrection. The Fourth Army could have had a worse outcome than being sent to the border.
So, has young Evgeny made up his mind to be at the forefront?
The Marshal is simply performing the duty of a soldier of Ursus, to protect her by standing where the danger is the greatest.
Our only problem lies with you, Your Imperial Highness.
The Marshal needs to know that you are willing to continue to cooperate.
Is this his notice to me?
A request made of a friend.
The Marshal values your friendship and trusts your wisdom. He believes that, even at a time like this, the interests of Bresk and the Fourth Army are closely aligned.
Besides, he is not asking you to do anything other than give the nod.
He seems to think it is an easy thing.
Does he think the Tsar a fool? Does he think Chairman Witte an idiot?
There is no middle ground... just as the sun cannot coexist with the night.
The Marshal understands your situation. He is happy to share in your burden.
In any case, there is considerable room for interpretation for the fall of an Emperor's Blade. The Marshal is glad to make the interpretation for you.
'The Fourth Army assisted Bresk in suppressing a rebellion in the Far North.' As long as you are willing to cooperate, that will be the only sentence in the Fourth Army's report to Grypherburg.
You do not even need to nod. The Marshal will accept silence.
No, that's not it... if the Marshal truly understands me, he should know that it was never Deity Grypherburg's inquiry I was concerned about...
The people who live upon my land, the people of Ursus... they are my only concern.
The Marshal understands your benevolence and vows that no one shall cross the line you have drawn.
Young, impatient Evgeny... I would not wish to see my dear friend put himself in danger.
Even if I do keep silent as he wishes, it would simply be one fewer pebble in his path. How does he intend to handle the mighty boulders?
That is not something you need concern yourself with. Be it boulders or anything else, the Marshal is confident he will overcome all foes that stand in the way of Ursine glory.
Do I take it that I have your response?
Field Marshal Evgeny sends his regards.
For the glory of Ursus.
For the glory of Ursus...
Are you alright, Ksenia? I can carry both of you, if necessary!
N-No, I'm fine...
I just don't understand... why do we have to be in such a hurry?

opts: I have a theory...
If the government has already learned about the ore vein incident...
There is only one explanation for the widespread silence in the Far North—
Ursus will not allow news of the vein exhaustion to get out.

lines.x: Look at the sky, Doctor!
Was it like this when we came in?
No... Am I seeing things?
Why that color... again?
Conspicuously red, the color of warning. You do not even need to look up to see it.
It begins to cover the gray skies, and everything below them.

opts: ...The army.
The warning light of the Ursine armies.
They're here.

lines.x: Why...?
Why would the army come to the Far North at a time like this? They shouldn't be here!
Doctor. High-energy Originium reaction on a large scale, five kilometers to the northwest. Someone is approaching, fast.

opts: We need to get back to the lab, now.
We need to let Amiya know at once.

lines.x: Are you alright, Ksenia? We're speeding up!
Yes, the lab...
We need to get back to the lab.
This way!
The emergency communications equipment is on the top-floor observation deck. The key is in the director's office—
Follow me!
(Whisper) Those footsteps didn't come from any of us.
(Whisper) Is there someone else here?

opts: There aren't many of them.
We don't have time.
We need to reach Amiya ASAP.

lines.x: Sorry, there's no emergency lighting here. I've lit a candle.
Yes, here's the communications equipment... I've never used it. Not sure how long its range is.
It's an Ursine military communicator.

opts: You've seen one before?

lines.x: Raidian has something similar. She helped the Ursine army's research into such equipment years ago. Looks like they've put it into production already.
If it's what I think it is, then it's a form of high-performance, large-area broadcasting equipment.
It's equipped with a high-powered Arts unit, roughly the equivalent of a hundred standard military communicators.
It was designed to broadcast urgent messages to military facilities in a large area. Experts at perceptive Arts, such as Raidian, are also able to receive such messages.

opts: Isn't that a good thing?
Why do you look so anxious?

lines.x: We can't specify the frequency to use.
If we use this device to send a message, the nearby Ursine army will receive it too, immediately revealing our position.
Doctor...

opts: ...Send the message.
Send the message, then move at once.

lines.x: Got it.
Tell me what message you want to send, and I'll operate it.

opts: The Ursine ore veins are near exhausted.

lines.x: Recorded. What's next?
Look out!
Whoa—!
Wh-Why are they attacking the lab?

opts: At least one army is intervening in the Far North.
They're trying to enforce a blackout—

lines.x: Message recorded!
Sending—

opts: Let's get out of here! Now!

lines.x: Doctor, Ksenia, grab on to me tightly.
We will—
Hello... hello—!
Damn. Did we make it in time?
You were pretty tough when you came for me, monster. Don't die here!
Do you hear me? Do you hear—
...Doctor?
(Yawns)
Look sharp. We're on shift for another two hours.
Blyat... not a damn mangler in this ice and snow.
Hey... did you hear something?
What—
The soldier turns around, only to see a crossbow bolt punching through his friend's skull.

Huh?
Ah! Ahhhh—!
Rankin! We got the ones below! Let's go up!
Wait! Don't be rash! Come back!
The miners are gathered under the outpost, gazing at the flashing warning light above their heads.
Watch your heads!
Mmph! Ugh!
They saw us! They're pulling the ladder!
We'll have to do this the hard way.
Take all the explosives and come with me, Team 3. We're blowing the outpost door, whatever it takes.
Don't stop if I fall. Take my place.
Team 4, Team 5, keep shooting and cover us!
The rest of you, don't let a single soldier get away.
You got it!
On my mark—
The searchlight catches them, and it is immediately followed by a hail of projectiles.
Rankin continues to push forward towards the outpost, raising his crossbow and taking aim at the vexing searchlight above their heads.
Argh—!
Suddenly, Rankin feels a sharp pain on his forehead, and the left side of his field of vision is covered in red.
Blood streams down. He hears a ringing in his head.
Rankin? Rankin!
Not a step back! With me! Push forward!
Damn it. We're dead anyway if we go back! Might as well take a few of them down with us!
The cries around him seem so distant. He can hear the sound of crossbow bolts finding flesh. He can hear the sound of bodies hitting the floor.
He must not fall. Not when victory is so close.
He wills his limbs to move with the last bit of strength he has left, once again raising his crossbow, aiming at that loathsome light above. He pulls the trigger.
They're weakening! Keep up!
Now, ready—
The door's blown! Charge!
The miners' voices feel more and more distant. It is all Rankin can do to keep up.
No one notices him in this battle of life and death.
He leans against the rubble and heads upwards, one small step at a time.
His senses are returning, and his body is getting used to the pain.
He walks over bodies that were alive just a moment ago. The spiral staircase leads to an aperture at the top, where the most beautiful aurora of the Ursine night shines through.
He remembers sitting in the mine elevators, gazing up at the only light above.
Finally, the last step. He finds himself standing amidst the aurora, beneath the stars, with friends.
Rankin?! Are you alright??
...I'm fine.
Rankin wipes away the blood on his forehead and takes a look at his brothers and sisters around him.
Half of us didn't make it...
...But we did it.
We did it...
In the distance, Deity Grypherburg remains hidden behind the thick taiga. The vast, frozen icy lake seems to taunt them for their foolish challenge.
We climbed from the dark mines to the surface, and now we stand atop the Army's outpost.
We will cross the frozen lake and forest, and plant our flags in even more places.
