Rankin's eyes burn bright as he tries to communicate his vision to the others.
Their faces are bright with the joy of victory too. They have waited for this day for far too long.
Nikto, we finally—
Rankin turns around, but does not see the familiar figure.
...Nikto?
*pant*... *pant*...
I ran away when the first bolt left the crossbow.
It's no shame to be afraid. It is only the most foolish of fools who do not know fear.
Rankin is mad. Everyone who follows him is mad.
I wanted to pull them back. But if they want to die, let them.
I can't die yet.
From cobbler to tutor, to being adopted by a count. I didn't do all that just so that I could die in a place like that.
Die, and it's all over.
I must survive.
Volgrad is just ahead...
...Start over, Nikto.
Blyat...
What the bloody hell is that...?
A torch!
We should light a torch, so that the others will know we've succeeded and come to meet up with us!
Yes... you're right.
Light the fire.
A dull thud coming from the distance shakes the icefields, and makes their eardrums and hearts tremble.
What's that?!
O-Over there!
They look in the direction that he is pointing, and see a massive battleship rising on the horizon, so massive it is hard to tell whether it is a ship or a nomadic city.
The taiga is leveled in an instant, and the stars are veiled by red light.
Snow and tree trunks are torn to pieces and sucked into the sky.
Only now...
Only now do they understand what they are dealing with.
Ursus.
They are dealing with Ursus.
I see...
So this is what it is...
Raidian?
I just got a message on a public channel.
It says that the Ursine ore veins are near exhausted...
The Armies are trying to cover it up. At least one army is already here in the Far North...!
Did the Doctor's team send the message?
Uncertain, but likely. This is a warning from the Doctor.
The mines becoming exhausted... could it be...?
But I don't understand. Ursus wants to cover it up? Do they plan on killing everyone in the Far North who knows about it?!
...I knew it.
The rumors of the ore veins running dry were true... That's the source of all the unusual events. That's what they're trying to hide!
You don't look surprised.
It was the worst-case scenario that I projected, until now.
But now, there is reason to believe it's true. And I have no doubt some of the Army brass are capable of doing such things...
The Far North is the most dangerous place on Terra right now. We must leave, immediately.
We haven't made contact with the Doctor yet—
They sent us a warning. They're in a better position to be informed than we are.
There's no guarantee that we would find them if we went back now. We would only be wasting our time.
Mon3tr can protect the Doctor. They're less likely to be targeted than a large group.
But if the Armies have come to cover this up on the government's behalf, how can we be sure that Grypherburg is a safe place to go?
You still don't trust me.
...We do need to make sure that...
'Kal'tsit.'
The name once again came to the government's attention not long ago. This mysterious figure has left too many unusual traces on Ursus, and across all of Terra.
Information about her is considered top-secret in Ursus. For this reason, some of us harbor hope that she can answer certain questions that Ursus is facing right now.
...Who are you, really?
Perhaps a re-introduction is in order...
Eloysius Vasilyevna Gorchekova, Lieutenant of the Tsar's Battalion, Ursine Imperial Guard. I answer to... Chairman Islam Witte of the State Council.
My orders are to investigate reports of mutated Infected and associated incidents in the Central Mining District of the Far North, and...
...To make contact with Rhodes Island.
I know it's a little late to claim sincerity, and it's true that I had intended to keep this a secret.
Whether you believe it or not, however, I can tell you that I could have dealt with you in Bresk, if I had any ill intent.
This is not the sort of cooperation that Rhodes Island seeks, but I can understand your position.
Let's set the argument aside for now... what's your plan, Eloysius?
I have vehicles on the southwest shore of Lake Neva, about ten kilometers from here.
It's not much, but it should be enough to take everyone away from here.
I can hide them in a village outside Grypherburg, then find a way to get you inside the city under the government's protection.
Mantra said that the people here are still under the influence of the Yurodstvo. We need to free them from its control first.
She can find the Yurodstvo, but not a way to deal with it?
I have no desire to witness a tragedy, but my duty comes first. I must ensure that someone is alive to bring word back to Grypherburg.
If you insist on getting everyone out of here safely...
I can give you one hour, no more.
Understood. I will make it quick.
I think I've made a breakthrough.
Mantra carefully projects her perception into every corner.
She identifies one little fluctuation amidst all the numb, unmoving thoughts.
...Babushka...
Let's go... let's leave here... alright?
If you stand up, everyone will... follow.
We'll go south, to Deity Grypherburg, or... anywhere, just not...
Just not here.
...Okay?
Vetochki takes a knee before the illusion that she believes to be her grandmother. She takes the hands that do not exist to Mantra's eyes, and rubs her cheeks against them.
It is only when she sees the tears on the girl's face that Mantra realizes how frightening this trust is. The girl continues to do so, even without getting any response.
Mantra remembers Raidian's words.
'Vetochki's parents were executed for organizing a strike. Her grandmother is her only family now.'
'It seems as though everyone in the mining district knew Nadia, including the guards.'
'They sought her assistance more than they sought the medical team's. They sought comfort when they were in pain, insight when they were lost, and found a strange peace in her words.'
'...I should have noticed that something wasn't right. I thought she was just a respected old woman.'
Please, babushka...
Mantra watches the girl before her, and gently makes her own voice appear in the girl's mind.
She believes you will find peace here.
Once all hope is gone.
Who—
Vetochki.
Vetochki turns around to see the Phidia standing behind her.
Y-You know, my name? But I never, told you...
In this place, at least, you should be able to express yourself clearly.
But you're talking in the same way that you usually do, which means that you've not been seriously affected...
Perhaps there's still a way for you to wake up.
What?
Is Nadia your family?
Yes, she's... she's my babushka.
And my only, family.
Are you sure?
Of course I am. She's my, babushka.
Your babushka wants everyone to endure their pain.
So that they have no desire to live, nor choose to end their own pain and die.
No, no—babushka wouldn't!
She's just tired, like everyone... Everyone's just, tired.
We want to live. Of course we do! But there's always people, who don't want us to...
Always someone...
Who?
Th-The Patrol Units.
You defeated the Patrol Units.
Th-The guards, and, the people, in the lab.
They're not here.
I, I—
Who else doesn't want you to live?
The black... monster...
And... and...
Why? I... I can't remember...
Let me ask again.
Are you holding the hands of your family?
Turn around, Vetochki.
Vetochki does not want to turn around. As long as she does not do so, those cold and withered hands will forever remain the way they are.
Tell me. What do you see?
Don't turn around. Don't turn around. Don't turn around.
She keeps telling herself.
But she turned around.
Babushka is in front of her. Babushka is singing a lullaby.
I... I see Babushka Nadia.
No. Look more closely.
Babushka is in front of her. Her hands are cold.
What do you see?
I see... circle. I see—
Red.
Vetochki frantically shakes her head.
Why...
Why is it always red?
Babushka sitting, babushka standing, babushka, colors ever-changing.
Babushka lying in a pool of blood, deciding that Vetochki may die.
Brrr. Is this what autumn is like in Ursus? I would hate to see what they call winter.
An open-air party, with no lawn or picnic table? What are you inviting us here to see?
Entertainment you don't get in Kazimierz, no matter how rich you are.
A few lives and deaths, big deal. I doubt those miners we saw on the way could give us a show that measures up to knightsports.
The knights fight every year, but Breskenoy hasn't seen a single death sentence in a decade under the rule of the current Grand Duchess! This is a truly rare event.
Death sentence? Who's the criminal?
A miner couple caught in the act, plotting to organize an Infected strike.
My, my, how awful...
Yes. It was such a horrendous crime that the Grand Duchess apparently wept when she signed the death warrant.
But there is no precedence for humane death sentences in these parts. It's easy to bury them in the nearby swamps, but it would be such a waste of a rare death sentence, wouldn't it?
Which is why we're here.
The procedures start with whipping. I heard paying guests like us even get to pick the whip, add blades to it, soak it in brine...
What if they died sooner than planned and their rocks exploded in front of us? I had this dress made just for this trip, you know.
That won't be a problem! Their infection isn't that bad. It would have taken them years to die if they didn't commit their crime.
As for the party and picnic that you asked about, you'll see it soon enough.
Now, let's get ourselves warm by the fire.
Are they still breathing?
Th-The woman's still babbling treason...
Is the man dead?
Cut his tongue out yesterday.
Why not cut the woman's too?
Th-The guests yesterday said no.
They wanted to hear her scream—
Did she?
No! Not even once. She keep repeating her 'petitions', saying things like 'for the other children who died in the mines'...
Couldn't understand a word of it! She must be mad.
Wouldn't have tried to start a strike if she wasn't.
*sigh* Cut the woman's tongue too.
Yes, sir!
And that kid. She was standing there yesterday too, wasn't she?
Yes. The constable wanted her to stand there and watch, until her parents breathe their last.
Then send her back to the quarters once they're dead.
I'm afraid she has to go down to the mines with her mom's lamp and ID. She was born in a mine, you know, which makes her a miner too.
...Does she have a lamp? Do you see a lamp in her hands?
N-No, sir!
Then send her back to the quarters once it's done, mudak!
Vetochki... here, take these. Butter, minced meat, chocolate... Good girl.
Uh...
D-Don't get any wrong ideas! It wasn't my husband who sold your parents out! I earned these myself, pushing the carts in the mines!
There's only you and your babushka now. Take care of her, alright?
Tell her she doesn't need to return the scissors she borrowed. Come find me if you ever need anything...
I'll do what I can to help.
*sigh* My kid would be around your age, if she had lived.
She said some other things, but I never answered.
Was it because I couldn't talk, or because I had already walked away and went home?
All I know is that I saw red—
Red dripping on greyish-white plastic.
I thought I was back outside at that place again... but no.
It was babushka.
Argh—ahhhh!
Ugh...
Yesterday, and today. I learned that the red inside a human body isn't endless. It will dry up. It makes people hurt. It brings death.
So I tried to stop the red that was flowing out, with my hands, like the game that I played with my friends when I was little.
'Whoever picks up the most water has the most happiness.'
'Whoever bags the most winds has the most freedom.'
*Sob* *sob*—
No—no, no!
I can't keep it.
I must keep it.
I can't—
Babushka...
Babushka.
The cold body sinks into the plastic, into the mud, before my eyes. I look up and see 'it'.
A pale, standing shadow.
I cried—

lines.st: 'Babushka Nadia?'

lines.x: I'm here.
...You told me to, endure, and forgive.
To survive, yes.
Must we still, endure?
Endure, and share, other pain, only leads to death now.
Do we endure... for nothing?
Desire anything, and you will only sin. Your suffering soul needs eternal peace.
Come, child. Come into my embrace.
...Did you forgive too?
I forgave.
We must accept the life that Ursus has granted, as we accept the death that Ursus has granted. We must not choose. We must not struggle.
Did you forgive? For killing mom, and dad... your own, child.
Yes, I forgive.
'I forgive'?
No...
Then... no.
You're not Babushka Nadia.
Babushka wouldn't, forgive like this. Not forgive for peace.
She won't forgive. She won't endure. She's dead... buried.
You're not her.
I am.
I am—
I am?
■■, submit to ■■...
Or... ■■ destruction.
Step back, Vetochki!
Sh-She's not! She's not, Babushka Nadia.
No, she's not.
But if, she's not, babushka...
Who is she?
The light of Arts floats around Mantra. She has made the foe reveal its true colors.
But it is not yet over. She must continue to build a barrier in the cognitive space, to isolate herself and the entity before her from other people.
In the meantime, she continues to communicate the truth.
'Yurodstvo.'
This is the Yurodstvo that you asked about. It is a demonic creation that has been given this name.
Perhaps it was once human. Now, it is a far cry from the concept of human.
I don't understand...
You don't have to.
I never understood its true nature, even though there was a chance, however minute, that I might have become it.
You saw it at the wrong time and called to it. That's all.
The wrong time?
So... it was wrong, from the beginning?
It's not your fault, Vetochki.
I'll take over from here.
Using Originium Arts is not the hard part of the combat.
Red and black mist fill the air. The white silhouette within does not speak or move, not even the fringes of its clothes.
It does not dodge or fight back, simply existing there in silence, allowing the barriers around it to wither away.
A void, a vortex that devours everything.
You are...
The scenes in the cognitive realm continue to flicker. They return to where they began.
A wilderness of unknown name or location. One could call it—
Sargon.
Why... why would this happen...?
Catastrophe has destroyed our home, why must war take our lives as well?
Padishah, silent and wise...
Hear my prayer, and comfort...
My pain.
...Futile.
Search as you might in my consciousness, you will never create a vision that makes me choose to sleep in the void.
You ■■... Padishah.
A Padishah. This is your past.
Your people sought comfort from you, but you would not even grant them an illusion, though it would have taken you little effort.
It's not comfort, it's just a lie.
A sweet dream cannot shaken the ancient, corrupt royalty.
I left that place, fled of my own accord.
Indeed, you are not shaken.
Your voice...
You cannot become the source of the voice, Yurodstvo. You cannot become... 'me'.
Because I cannot speak, and because you're simply—
'An Echo.'
'People see in me the reflection of their perception and expectation.'
That's how you come to be.
But to you, and to my own folly...
I have no expectation.
You are strong...
You have not been tried or baptized, yet you wield power almost the same as mine.
Yet you have neglected your power, this gift... you should have used it to proselytize and lead your people.
What made you leave your palace? What made you give up on power and become weak?
I realized...
Knowledge is more lasting than authority. The truth is stronger than lies.
And you...
You have spurred them onto a path of destruction, to die upon this frozen soil.
Why?
Soft laughter ripples through the air.
It is revelation. It is ■■. It is proselytization. It is sacrifice.
The land is about to undergo drastic changes. Those who are not strong will be destroyed.
Therefore, I nurtured a spark, making it strong, making it spread.
It will burn all that is impure, and all shall be born again within.
Mantra is certain she can kill it. In the realm of the mind, it will take little more effort than snuffing out a candle.
But what will killing it do? She is unsure. Besides, is there victory to be gained when your foe never engaged?
Stop wasting time, Yurodstvo.
Cease your influence on the people here, and let them leave with me.
Otherwise, Ursus will soon need a new Yurodstvo.
No... no...
Your past is thick and long, ■■.
But it must be there, somewhere...
Your pain, the pain that you ■■ hide from...

lines.st: 'Accept it.'

lines.x: There was no time for Mantra to react. A split second of hesitation is enough to cause a crack in the cognitive realm that she used all her strength to maintain.
She realizes her error almost instantly, but countless responses are already coming from outside the realm.
I accept.
I... I accept.
I accept. I have long accepted.
I can eat straw, moss, drink snow water with gravel mixed in, until my stomach is full, and I shall not hunger. Not anymore.
See? Death is not more terrible than living.
At the end of her vision, Yurodstvo is white and slender, not a trembling apparition, nor a hunched old woman.
Whatever its appearance, it will continue to exist, as long as a single person believes in it.
The cause is not here. It is somewhere else.
Time starts flowing again. Mantra looks around, and her reason and emotion fight a war in her head, arguing about whether to try to fix the error, or beat a retreat.
Soon, she makes up her mind.
Elysium.
Right here!
I need you to use thermal imaging to mark the location from the outside. Connect me to every miner here.
Can you do it?
Of course—
You got it!
The woman's cold voice appears in every miner's head.
The instant that the connection is made, she realizes that these people can no longer endure any more shocks from mental Arts.
She can only keep out Yurodstvo for so long. Time is of the essence.
Perhaps this will be cruel to you. But I believe it's necessary to reveal the truth.
If this exodus began out of despair and manipulation, then you should at least decide your own ending.
Who's... talking?
How did I end up back here...?
We're not here! We ran away with Rankin—we cut out the chips and got away!
This isn't real.
You're half-correct. This is not the truth of the present, but of the past.
You killed the one on the ground. Yes or no?
Yes, yes! We killed him... Rankin killed him!
I thought Rankin was a hero. He said he would take us to a place where the Infected won't be looked down on, but he abandoned us in the end...
So you validated the truth of this scene.
I was there! I saw it. We killed and defiled our own soul...
Look again.
Tell me. Who is it?
Nadia, Vetochki's babushka. Everyone in the mines respected her, even the guards.
You respected her too. In the way that you respect your elders, or in the way that you respect a statue of a god?
There's little difference, if you live to her age in the mines.
She stood among you. She joined the revolt.
No, she didn't. She wasn't there... no one would ask an old woman to fight.
She's here? Who is she looking at? Is she mourning the fallen? Why would she mourn a murderer?
Because it was she who killed him.
What?
Rankin and your group never killed the last one who fell.
What killed him was his hubris, and the one who created the illusion, making you blame yourself for his death.
'Yurodstvo.'
Vetochki stands by the door. She wants to leave, but her feet will not move. She wants to look straight ahead, but she does not have the courage.
She can only look down at the watch in her hand, watching the hands tick and tock, moving to the next number.
What time is it? Which one is the hour hand, and which one is the minute hand? What about the extra one?

lines.st: Mama said not to sleep in the snow. You'll get cold and pass out, and heavy snow can kill.
Rankin said not to doze off in the mines. You'll faint if the air is stale, and air that you can't see will burn.
But this isn't the snow or the mines. Can we sleep here, rest here?

lines.x: Who can...
Save us?

lines.st: Babushka is gone. It's not Babushka Nadia standing there.
No...
Not here. Rest, but not in someone else's home. Sleep, but not because you've lost the strength to live.

lines.x: Don't sleep. Don't rest.
We'll...
Save ourselves.
She steadies the hastily-forged shield. The bell that she rang to warn the others to evacuate the mines hangs from it.
Vetochki swings her pickaxe and rings it again.
...So she's really the Yurodstvo?
I heard you worship her like the Yurodstvo.
She's the Yurodstvo... she saved us! It all makes sense now.
She comforted us, shared our pain... she was always with us.
Where were you back then?
Then she should save you now.
The miners look to the Yurodstvo. It stands silent like a statue.
Maybe we sinned too much.
No.
The victimizer, the one who deceived and caused pain, wants the victim to endure and submit. It has always been so. You do not see any path other than to obey.
It's not your fault.
Do you mean that we can still be saved?
Oh, shut it, Vasya. Does it even matter now?
Yes, you can be saved.
If you leave here and continue your journey.
Choose to live, when living may be worse than dying.
Hah. Get lost.
I thought you were going to say something new. If that's all, then I would rather die with my corpse intact, and I'm sure everyone else—
Bells? Who's ringing the bells?
It's Vetochki...
Sounds like someone has made a choice.
Vetochki silently rings the bell, even though it does not sound the way it used to.
She rings it again and again, until someone stands up.
Is our shift over?
Our shift's over.
I want to leave here.
I want to go home...
More and more people stand up from the ground, step away from the walls, and their figures begin to drown out the white at the end of the house.
The Yurodstvo's figure trembles, passing through Mantra and the people standing up in the house.
It comes before Vetochki.
Child.
Vetochki looks down and does not answer the call.
She continues to ring the bell, until everyone is roused, and a dazed smile appears on their faces.
Spasibo, Vetochki.
Go back, Vetochki. Don't stay here too long.
Vetochki—
No, I...
I'm not going back.
Vetochki?
Vetochki gazes straight ahead—her companions stop smiling, and the figure of Yurodstvo is now nearly transparent.
She said, no.
We're not going back.
We'll leave, and we'll live.
Until living, isn't the only thing, we care about, anymore.
They look so much better now.
Are you alright, Mantra?
This is Mantra.
The communication has concluded. The miners have been freed from the Yurodstvo's control.
You defeated the Yurodstvo?
I wouldn't say that. It was the result of their own effort. The Yurodstvo has disappeared, and I was unable to track it.
We have more important matters to attend to now.
The Armies...
They're here.
Report, Elysium.
No hostiles within five klicks in the direction we're traveling. We can stay the course.
The road ahead should be easier once we're out of this stretch of tundra.
Good to hear. Thank you, Elysium.
There's bad news too, though. If the enemy isn't in front, they're probably behind us.
Watch your six, Amiya.
I will.
The cannonfire is drawing closer...
The group can't move any faster.
Even worse, we have no idea where they are.
We need more information, Amiya. At least, we need to know how they're moving.
You want to intercept military comms? Can you do that?
I'm familiar with Ursine military encryption methods, but it's been a while and it'll take time for me to identify the frequency that they're using.
Why are you looking at me like that?
Alright alright. Want me to leak even more classified information than I already have? Well, I can't guarantee it will help...
But if we both live to stand before a military tribunal, I'll testify that all you did was purely in self-defense.
Your jokes could use some work, Sergeant.
I never said it was a joke. Please, consider lowering your expectations.
They're low enough. Just tell me the frequency, then... 'keep quiet.'
Uh, I'll write it down.
Found it. Thank you.
Tracing signal... switching to active... ship-mounted radio? Let me see...
'...rebellious Infected miners at large in the Far North...'

lines.st: 'The Infected have engaged in large-scale massacres, causing significant civilian casualties. A large number of armed Infected are now heading to Deity Grypherburg.'
'The Fourth Army hereby answers the call of duty to suppress the insurrection in order to protect Deity Grypherburg and the people.'
'The operation will be carried out swiftly. Citizens are advised to remain calm and cooperative.'

lines.x: What? Their shamelessness leaves you at a loss for words?
I'm unsurprised.
Sounds like they'll at least avoid the nomadic cities, but we should monitor civilian communications just in case... anyone have a map?
Not on me. I'll get it—
I have it here.
Can I, listen to, what you're listening to?
I want to know, about the other, mines...
Are we, the only ones, who left? Were there, others...?
What they say on the radio isn't true, Vetochki.
I-I know!
Just wanted to know, if they're safe. Can we?
It's true that radio broadcasts from the mines could be a source of information. Thank you, Vetochki.
Lieutenant Gorchekova, please mark on the map any nomadic cities in the path of the Armies' heading.
Wait... the signal has been changed...
Military channel. That one just now was on civilian channels. Now they're sharing internal communications with other units.

lines.st: 'Central Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'

lines.x: What...?
'Purged?'
They're purging...
What?
Don't listen, Vetochki!
Raidian tries to cover her ears, but the girl shakes her head.
The next message comes through after a brief silence.

lines.st: 'Satellite City's Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'
'Olonets Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'
'Volgrad Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'

lines.x: Names of places mixed with static are sent back one after another from the terminal. Some are familiar, some are not. No one dares imagine what is happening on the other side.
A name. Just a name.
That's enough. Turn it off.
We're not getting any more information... turn it off.
No... no...
I want to know... I want, to know.
They're... our friends.

lines.st: 'Ural Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'
'Kras Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'
'Onega Mining District purged.'
'Acknowledged.'

lines.x: The terminal's playback is like a bland, looping song.
The aurora sways in the night sky, a beautiful curtain that shuts out the screaming happening elsewhere on this tundra.
It is a long night.
...I understand, Your Imperial Majesty. I always have.
I understand your kindness. But no one has ever been respected for kindness...
What will happen to a nation, when it loses the energy that is its lifeblood?
Either it becomes livestock whose survival depends on the whims of others, or it must plunder the others first.
This is not the desire of the Armies... it is the desire of Ursus.
Ursus must not be weak. If she becomes weak, we must cut away what is weak, or the others will fall upon her in an instant.
All of this to say...
Silence can be such a difficult thing.
Grand Duchess Elisabeth tosses the letter with the State Council's seal into the fireplace.
The flames rise with a soft hiss, then all is quiet again.
Vetochki... calm down and listen to me.
Don't be afraid... don't give up. We can make it. We'll take everyone out of this place.
I want to leave...
But, 'massacre'... killing people? They're trying to... shut everyone up?
They used to do it, with butter, and coffee. Then, with whips, and death.
Papa and Mama, didn't keep quiet, so they're... dead.
Vetochki...
But what if, we're dead, even if, we keep quiet?
The mining districts, what we heard... they're all dead? But we're, the only ones, who know.
Shouldn't more people know, before we're, silenced too?
They can't, kill everyone.
Can they?
You're right. But sending a message would reveal our position to the nearby Army...
We...
Eloysius swallows her words. She wanted to be the voice of cold reason, but she cannot say the words.
Vetochki is right.
We can't let them bury everything that happened here.
You've just been through a hard fight, Mantra.
There's no need to worry. It's my duty.
But I can't NOT worry...
You didn't want to come to the north, or reveal your Arts. There must be reasons for that, whether it's a past you didn't want to expose, or...
Rhodes Island won't place undue burdens on our operators. I don't know what you went through here, but I know it takes great courage to overcome it.
Thank you for your understanding, Amiya. But I no longer need to avoid it, for I've left it behind.
We'll reveal the tragedy that happened here, and expose their plot to all.
A-Alright!
And we have a witness.
Let's begin.
Calibration complete... how are you feeling?
Good.
We'll expand the area, then. Raidian?
One moment. Maybe I can make use of the magnetic storms. The electrical signals carried in the noise...
How are you feeling now, Mantra?
I can hear more now.
'More' is a vague answer. She not only hears more, she hears further, hears more details—
She hears the battleship's tracks rumbling across the tundra, the screams that not even Originium engines and guns can drown.
She hears parents speaking southern Ursine dialects, kissing their children goodnight; couples whispering words of love on the balcony before going inside, the curtains left open.
She hears a radio program in the Northern Tundra, interrupted from time to time by the magnetic storms; an old man falling asleep stroking a cloudbeast in his arms in a nursing home...
She feels as though she can hear everything, and she knows she has to interrupt.
'Comrades—'
Mantra suddenly realizes that she should not be the one saying the words.
At least, not the only one.
Vetochki.
Y-Yes?
These are your words.
Say them with me.
But I, don't speak, clearly—
You can do it.
I, I—
'Comrades in the mines and cities.'
'C-Comrades, in the mines, and cities.'
'We come from the Far North.'
'We come from... the Far North.'
'We are being slaughtered.'
'We are b-being...'

lines.st: 'Slaughtered.'

lines.x: Comrades, in the mines, and cities. We are hunted miners from the Far North.
We are still alive, but we are being slaughtered.
We did not betray Ursus.
Ursus abandoned us.
I-I did it!
You did it.
Now...
I might need some help.
'Dispatch speaking.'
'Target acquired. They're moving toward Lake Neva.'
'Cease fire and surround the target coordinates.'
'Coordinates sent to your terminals.'
Are you out of the woods, Raidian?
Scout team here. We're at the rendezvous point.
The first batch of vehicles are here. We'll need to wait for the second batch to arrive.
Sorry, I didn't expect things to get so urgent.
Have you tracked more of the Armies' movements, Raidian?
Not yet... the military channel went dark after our broadcast. They're on guard now, and we're unable to get more information.
It's so quiet, it's making me uneasy...
Stay hidden, Amiya.
We'll be careful.
Stay in touch. How's Mantra?
Still exhausted. Vetochki and the others have made a stretcher for her—
Amiya.
Mantra! You shouldn't be 'talking'!
I'm awake. Tell Elysium that I can walk on my own.
No, not yet.
See? I told you so!
Remember how Blaze was bedridden for two weeks after getting injured on a mission? We didn't let her feet touch the ground for a single second.
So don't even think about it, dear Mantra.
...The situation is different.
Okay, here's a compromise. Once you're through the woods, you can choose me or Elysium to be your crutch.
Is G-Grypherburg, on the other side, of the frozen lake?
I heard, the Infected, can't enter n-nomadic cities.
I never went to the cities before I got infected. I lived all my life in the villages, until a Catastrophe infected most of us.
But they said we didn't need to walk, right? There'll be vehicles, like the ones we found.
I think Nikto was from Grypherburg. Too bad he isn't here.
Say, what do you think Nikto and Rankin are doing, Vetochki? Could they have already made it to that village they were talking about?
M-Maybe.
I hope so.
Everyone, take cover, now!
An explosion—from the forest? Is the Army here?
I just sensed it... Another army's attacking from the east.
They know where we are. They're trying to force us in the direction of Lake Neva.
They're out of their minds! We're already in the Central Governorate. The Armies must know what that means!
'Deity Grypherburg is under threat... the Army... will eliminate the enemy.'
It's a public broadcast. They're telling Grypherburg that they're putting down a rebellion!
I see... this was their plan all along...

All this killing and herding... they're planning to reach the capital under the pretext of putting down a rebellion, and negotiate with the State Council by pointing their guns at the gates of Deity Grypherburg!
This was what they're trying to achieve... by sacrificing hundreds of thousands in the Far North.
Can you hear me, Amiya?
There are Army units to your east—
...The magnetic storms are interfering with comms. I can't get a good signal at this range.
The rest of the group will arrive soon. Eloysius, I need you here to get them on the vehicles and evacuate in an orderly manner.
What about you?
I must go back and point the way for them.
You're leaving me here with these vehicles? Aren't you worried that I would abandon you and flee to Grypherburg?
If that's your decision, I would appreciate a heads-up.
Rhodes Island will find a way.
You...
Hah... is this 'Rhodes Island' full of people like you?
No. They're much braver than I.
Your command, Sergeant.
What the hell is that thing behind us? It appeared out of nowhere—
(Stumbles)
Look out!
The Army is shelling indiscriminately. Should we find another path?
Can you reach Raidian, Elysium? The scout team might be in even more danger than we are!
Amiya! Where are you?
We're still in the forest on the eastern shores of Lake Neva! We're being fired upon by the Army—
They have reinforcements coming from the east. We must get through the lake as quickly as possible! Grypherburg is on the other side.
The Armies cannot set foot on the western shore of Lake Neva without permission. That's our only chance.
We need to clear Lake Neva before the Armies complete their encirclement—
Raidian? Raidian?!
Magnetic storm—communications—
—southwest—ice—
No lights, everyone. We'll have to cross the lake in darkness.
Go southwest... I'll point the way.
I understand. The stars, show the way—
Cannon shells fall like thunder, exploding in the middle of the crowd. The shockwave and shrapnel split the procession in two.
Dyadya Volya!
A crack appears in the ice, and some people have fallen into it.
Aaagh!!
The ice is still cracking—we can't get through!
Go back! Now! Don't come this way!
They're getting closer!
Can you reach Raidian?!
We got separated. We have some straggling miners we need to pick up, to the northwest of where the shells fell—
Roger.
On my way.
Argh—ugh...
Darya, your leg, it's bleeding... I'll wrap it up!
I'll help—
...No, Vetochki. Leave me here.
I have flint and kindling. Give me a torch and go. I'll buy you what time I can.
I'll carry you, Darya!
Don't... don't give up.
Yes, don't give up! The bleeding will stop soon. You can get further treatment once we're on the vehicles—
But those bastards won't wait! They're herding us like wild manglers. Don't you see?
If they just wanted to kill us, they could have burned every inch that we passed through, blown every building up.
Do you think they couldn't do it?
That's no reason to give up...
You don't understand. I was a hunter before they threw me into the mines. I know what some of the cityfolk like to do on hunting trips.
They corral their game in one place, kill a couple, and watch the rest struggle.
Honestly, I don't know how much time I can buy, but I have to try.
It's just a hole in my leg. I'll crawl if I can't walk. I can get a few dozen meters to the east.
Stop wasting time. Leave the torch and go.
Go!
One by one, one by one! We'll be here for fifteen minutes, there's no need to—
—push...
The operator falls quiet. No one is pushing, no one is squeezing.
They just keep walking forward silently.
...I've never seen an evacuation so orderly.
Not even the famously disciplined First Army could do this.
That's a good thing, right?
It's not normal, is it? It's as though they don't have survival instincts.
Alright. Maybe I'm overthinking it.
And you, aren't you worried about your captain in the rear?
Our captain is the most experienced of us by far.
The operator stands on tiptoes, trying to see past the end of the procession that is indistinguishable from the pitch-black ice, looking for her captain.
All she sees is the bright red in the distance.
Did they really stop there...?
The enemy is shelling the ice!
I couldn't reach the driver. Someone needs to tell her to keep the vehicle running and be ready to roll at a moment's notice.
Do you read me?!
R-Roger!
You should get on, Eloysius. I'll take care of things here—
No, you go first and talk to the driver. For your captain.
I—
I'm staying here.
It was a torch that distracted the Army.
It began with a single torch that someone at the end of the procession raised.
No one heard if he said anything. No one asked why he did it. He simply stopped, lit his torch, and began to walk in the opposite direction.
Who lit the torch? I thought they said no lights—
It's Kian.
Kian? The one who made flint for everyone? I saw he gave some firewood to Darya just now.
It's him. He's walking back. But that's where all the soldiers are! Is he trying to get himself killed?
He's doing the same thing that Darya is doing.
But we're so close to safety—
I have a flint too, and some firewood.
What are you doing?
They're shelling the ice. They won't let us go. If we don't do something...
We all die.
But I want to live! I want to go to Grypherburg!
We would have died in the mines, remember? Like all other Infected in Ursus.
But if we can help the folks on the front lines survive, and the young ones who came to our aid...
People will remember what happened here long after we old-timers are gone.
One torch passes its flame to another.
One miner forsakes his life, then a second, and a third...
One by one, the miners who have not yet boarded the vehicles raise their torches and walk in the opposite direction.
Towards Kian, towards Darya, towards the trail of footsteps that led here, and towards the impending threat.
Th-They can't... bury us.
Not anymore.
You're right. They can't bury us quietly anymore, not in the snow, in the mines, or in the lakes!
We'll take the torch from our brothers and sisters, and we'll light the way...
We'll make sure that all of Ursus sees—
And knows that we once lived.
The miners who chose to stay behind raise their torches high in the cold night.
They form a bright belt of fire on the ice, one that the gunners cannot miss.
'Ursus will not bury this night the way it has buried many others,' they firmly believe.
The red eyes gaze upon the firelight.
The guns continue to fire, and the ice continues to crack, sending more miners into the gaping crevasses.
They shout and keep their torches high to the very end, with only a slight trembling in their voices.
Of course they are afraid. Of course they are fearful.
But they keep their torches raised, trading their lives for the lives of those behind them.
The Rhodes Island personnel try to stop them, but no one listens.
They're still firing, Amiya! Eloysius's vehicles are full, and the next batch hasn't arrived yet—
Amiya!
Why... why are they doing this?
We have to stop them. We can't let—
I can't stop this many in my current state.
But there must be something we can do.
Mantra...
I can feel it. I can feel their...
Their... anger.
It's so powerful. It's like it's burning them to ashes.
No...
I won't let them.
This—
This slaughter has to stop!
Anger. The same anger.
An emotion that Amiya has always fought to suppress, one that she would never allow to run unchecked.
Anger lights up her eyes, ignites her heart, and flows to her fingertips.

lines.st: 'Even if I don't take revenge, even if I never hold a grudge, I still have the right to rage on forever!'

lines.x: The cerulean and black sword of wrath once again appears in her hand.
Ying Xiao—
A clean swing. Blue fire bursts from the tip of the sword, the highly concentrated energy drawing a wall of fire on the ice in the blink of an eye—another long and clear boundary.
On one side, the cracking ice and the shells repelled by the wall of fire.
On the other, the little Cautus and the torch-bearing Infected.
You...
Amiya points her sword at the shore, at the Army that has herded and slaughtered people like livestock.

lines.st: 'You go no further.'

lines.x: Blue fire rages at her sword-tip, a warning to the murderers, be it the guns, the soldiers, or the battleships. Until the flames cease, until the score is settled—
None shall pass.
'Sir, high-powered Originium signal, twelve o'clock, source unknown!'
'Slow down and observe.'
'Yes, sir.'
...Did they cease fire?
No, they just slowed down.
You've stopped some of their attacks, Amiya. Rendezvous ASAP.
The other vehicles will arrive soon! Let's get out of here!
I'm staying until everyone is out.
Wait... something's coming!
A vehicle's coming toward us at high speed from the west, but it's not one of ours—I think it's a truck!
They drove onto the ice!
On alert—
Raidian! Finally got this thing to work. It's us! Me, Doctor, Ksenia—

opts: And Lyudmila.
And Operator Crownslayer.
Rhodes Island...
Assemble.

lines.x: Amiya!
Doctor! Mon3tr! How did you—
And, Operator Crownslayer...?
Didn't have time to chat along the way. I have a lot of questions myself.
But get on first.

opts: We owe the Truckers' Union one.
I'll tell you about it later.
Is everyone alright?

lines.x: Won't disappoint you.
We'll catch up later. For now, we need to get out of here.

opts: Yes. Operator Mon3tr, Operator Crownslayer...
And Doctor, reporting in.

lines.x: After scanning the survivors on the ice, the lights of the outpost on the southwest shore of Lake Neva point to the Army on the opposite shore.
White light faces off against red light, and the shelling grows even sparser.
'All units, this is Dispatch.'
'All units, cease fire immediately. We have an urgent request from Deity Grypherburg.'
'Repeat, all units, cease fire immediately.'
'Please acknowledge.'
'Yes, sir.'
Looks like it's stopped... for real this time.
And everyone's here.
Thank goodness...

opts: Yes...
Looks like we made it.

lines.loc: Veteran's Home, Deity Grypherburg

lines.x: ...This is my report on the Far North Incident. I will submit my written report within one week.
Thank you, Lieutenant. But I still have questions.
What you've mentioned—getting into the mining district, investigating the miners, evacuating them under the Armies' gunfire... those are merely the process.
An event must have cause, process, and result, just like how crops grow from seeds, and yield fruits.
I apologize for my poor presentation, Your High Excellency, but I have mentioned both the cause of the event and how we handled the aftermath.
All scattered information. You have described the details, but you have not seen the big picture. How can the reader understand what the writer fails to perceive?
Have you forgotten how to write a report in the five years since you graduated from the military academy? Or do you want me to submit a fragmented, incomplete report to His Imperial Majesty?
I can forgive your youthful errors, but Ursus cannot afford to wait.
I apologize. I will fill in the gaps in the written report—
We do not have the time to address the consequences of your omissions. The price of trial and error is too high.
I need you to tell me, right here, right now. What role do you think Grand Duchess Elisabeth played? Were there signs of her association with the Armies? As for the Infected that we rescued...
Can they truly be of help to us?
I'm not sure I have sufficient information to talk about it in an informed manner...
You experienced the events first-hand. No one is more informed than you.
Speak, child. I will listen.
...I believe that the Grand Duchess was simply going with the flow.
She had learned about the Royal Guard's death and the miners' escape before we even planned to enter the mining district.

All she needed to do was to let a little information out, and the Fourth Army would have an excuse to 'clean up', and proceed towards the Central Governorate under the pretext of quelling the rebellion.
This way, the Grand Duchess could cover up the death of a Royal Guard on her watch, and the Army can deploy on the borders of the Central Governorate... as it does now.
As for why the Army is doing this... I believe you know better than anyone.
They mistake the Tsar's benevolence for weakness. They will pay the price.
I concur with most of your speculation, Elotta. But there is one thing you are missing—the Yurodstvo.
It disappeared. I could not confirm whether it was real or not, and I do not even feel the need to dig any deeper. After all... we witnessed the burning of the last Yurodstvo with our own eyes.
You do not believe it was the Yurodstvo who appeared in the Far North?
I simply believe you know more than I do.
The troubling madman... a poisonous relic of an older era. I will get to the bottom of it. Ursus does not need a Yurodstvo anymore.
Now, let us get back to the topic at hand. Were their goals accomplished, Lieutenant?
They failed to hush up news of the ore vein exhaustion. Which leads us to your other question—
Can the Infected that we rescued truly be of help to us? In fact, they've already helped.
The belt of torches on the ice awakened all the outposts around Lake Neva on the Grypherburg side. Every household in Ursus has heard their message.
'Ursus abandoned them! Who will be next?' Everyone asks the same question: the newspapers, intercity comms, late-night radio...
The miners in the mines ask, the workers in the city ask; wealthy citizens ask, poor vagabonds ask; soldiers ask, students ask.
The Grand Duchess expected the miners to die in the Far North. But they survived, testifying to the exhaustion of the ore veins, and exposing the inadequacy of the reforms—
Slow, gradual improvement is not enough for the pressing needs of Ursus's development.
We must act, before even more people are fed into the hungry machine of the Empire.
You said yourself that Ursus can no longer afford to wait...
You're digressing, Lieutenant.
You said Rhodes Island helped the Infected miners send the message.
Yes. Right now, however, we should focus on—
What do you think of Rhodes Island?
...We should be vigilant.

They demonstrated military-level coordination during the tracking and evacuation process, and they possess a natural advantage in their understanding of the Infected. There are plenty of reasons to be vigilant.
Or perhaps, their strength is something that we can leverage.
As far as I am aware, Rhodes Island has no permanent presence in Ursus. Perhaps we can invite them to set up a branch office in Grypherburg or a nearby city...
Perhaps.
The Grand Duchess might strike first.
She's not one to be rash. Did you mention the name 'Kal'tsit' to the Rhodes Islanders?
Yes. They said that Kal'tsit previously served as a physician at Rhodes Island, but had died in an accident.
Are you certain?
I've dispatched a unit to the scene of the incident to ascertain. From what we know so far, there is no reason to think otherwise.

lines.loc: Wilderness, Central Governorate, Ursus

lines.x: So... Kal'tsit is dead.
The name that Chairman Witte extracted from the Chernobog lab files is no longer of use.

opts: You're looking for Kal'tsit.
Why?

lines.x: Because of the Sarcophagus, of course. Ursus needs its energy.

opts: You know where it is?

lines.x: I don't know the specifics, unfortunately.

opts: But Witte gave you a name: Kal'tsit.
He wanted you to find Kal'tsit through us.
Witte trusts you.

lines.x: Of course. Otherwise, he wouldn't have sent me alone—
...Sent me to the Far North alone.
Yes, he trusts me. It's the duty of a soldier to follow orders, and it's not my place to complain, even if I should die in the Far North.
But it shouldn't be this way. Miners and civilians were massacred, but the Grand Duchess gets out unscathed, and the Army is able to deploy a stone's throw from Grypherburg with no consequences.
This isn't how things should be! How can I go back like this...?

opts: Lieutenant Gorchekova.
If I told you, there was a way to bring Kal'tsit back to life...
What would you do?

lines.x: What?
Are you joking? How is that possible? Is this Kal'tsit like the fae of legend, able to return to life whenever she wants?
Wait a moment. The Chairman did say that the secret police once believed Director Kal'tsit to have died in that incident...
But...
You just tell me like that? Did you think I would trust you the same way in return? You're not even Ursine—
What if some of us used to be?
You...
Even if you really do know nothing, we won't forget that you once stood with us there, helping evacuate the Infected.
We appreciate what you did, Lieutenant Gorchekova, even if you don't need our thanks. Am I right?
Tell me, and I'll listen, once again as a child of Ursus.
Alright, I knew I couldn't resist the chance to awaken a straying Ursine's identity.
What I know about the Sarcophagus is very limited...
—Lieutenant?
I'm sorry. I think I got distracted.
It's alright. Bad habits of the elderly. I can't stop myself when I start talking about the motherland and our future with the youth...
Perhaps you really do need rest. I've taken up too much of your time.
No, Your High Excellency, I was simply thinking about what you said—
Get some rest, Lieutenant.
Unlike the Far North, Grypherburg still sees some daylight this time of year. Do not forget to finish your report, once you've rested.

lines.st: 'As the mines begin to reopen gradually in the Northern Tundra, the Third Army casts its gaze towards the Central Governorate.'
'The Fourth Army continues its illegal presence on the border of the Central Governorate. All citizens are advised to respect the cordon and refrain from traveling to the Lake Neva area.'

'The First Army has begun to conscript students from dozens of universities in the Central Governorate, without authorization from the State Council. Student protests across the country are being bloodily suppressed.'
'The Second and Seventh Armies have been mobilized, in response to Grypherburg's request...'

lines.x: That's all you see in the papers these days. I've got a cousin going to school in the Central Governorate. Haven't heard from her in a while.
Did you hear? Uchitelnitsa Koshelna didn't go to Deity Grypherburg Imperial University. She went to the mines to teach the miners how to read!
That's so brave and selfless of her. I want to follow in her footsteps when I graduate...
I hope we'll see her again.
Don't listen to everything you read in the papers! It's all bad news to make us anxious and demoralize us.
The student council is holding a protest in Igor Memorial Park. Are you going?
Of course!
M-Me too!
There's a lot of movement in the military. Student and worker strikes are taking place all across the country, but they're being suppressed even swifter and more harshly than before...

Also, the Grypherburg police recently arrested a student from the Literature Department attempting to graffiti the grand hall of the State Council. I heard the Petrachevsky Group is soliciting donations and trying to get him released.
It's true, I checked with them. Every underground tabloid is spreading the news about him, and the words he wrote on the walls of the council hall.
The underground tabloids are printing far quicker than the censors can keep up.
Let's put that in the next letter to Sonya.
They should be on their way back to Grypherburg, come to think of it...
Write anyway. There are curfews everywhere. There's no guarantee they'll make it back on schedule.
Yes... I hope things go smoothly on their side.
They will.
Getting in wasn't that hard.
Just because I hacked the monitors doesn't mean you can let your guard down. They say the session has been suspended indefinitely, but they could also resume at any time.
The corridor leading to the meeting hall is still being patrolled four times an hour. Not counting the time it takes to get out, you have eight minutes to leave your mark.
Pee? Break something and take a piece?
...Can you do something that doesn't make so much noise and actually means something?
Well... I'll think about it.
Why do I have to dress like this, anyway? It's not like my normal clothes are all that conspicuous.
You're wearing a Grypherburg plumber's uniform. They don't have a seat on the council, but they have access to the council hall, and they're responsible for maintenance work throughout the building.
While there's no way for students like us—complete non-producers—to gain access to the council, much less gain any real influence.
Bah, why am I even explaining all this to you? I'm hanging up. You have six minutes.
Botani out.
Lokh.
What should I do? Six minutes...
The student in the plumber's uniform casts his gaze towards the end of the council hall, where the symbol of Ursine divine glory shines bright on the wall.
He thinks for a moment, then squints and pulls out a can of red spraypaint.
Brekin is not on duty today, but his colleague, who lives next door to him, sent his youngest son to his place and asked him to take his shift today, because he had a fever and a splitting headache.
Brekin, being the kind-hearted man he is, agreed. Scarcely could he imagine what he would see when he walked into the council hall—
Gosudar-batyushka—
The golden symbol on the wall is fully covered in red paint. Beneath it are words written with the same red paint, in the Ursine language—

lines.st: 'The victor displays the emblem of the vanquished for his vanity, but he will forever stand in the shadow of the vanquished, if he cannot cast it away.'
—Yakov Petrov, penned in prison in the autumn of 1076

lines.loc: Barren Farmlands, Ursus-Kazimierz Border

lines.x: Oh... Another sunny day. I don't seem to remember any winters ever being so warm.
How wonderful... As long as the wheat we're planting survives the winter and we don't hit a Catastrophe, we'll be self-sufficient next year!
It's a little too early to celebrate, after all I've been through...
Come on, have a little optimism, Babushka! There was a time we thought we'd have nowhere to go, that we'd just die in the barrens! But now, we have our own farm, a house, and hot porridge—
Huh? Who's over there...?
It's Miss Talulah! And Nine!
Miss Talulah, are you helping us pull the weeds?
Talulah, she's talking to you.
Oh—
Yes, that's right. Am I doing it wrong?
I saw everyone doing it this way, and the weeds here have grown pretty tall. So tall you can't see the wheat.
No, no, you're doing it right. Pulling the weeds out will help make sure the wheat gets enough nutrients to survive the winter—
You're such a quick learner!
Pff.
You've already pulled so many, and you haven't hurt a single wheat bud! Looks like you don't need my help anymore.
You taught us well. How's Iakov, by the way?
The new medicine he's been taking is very effective, thanks to you! He used to writhe in pain until he fell out of bed at night, but no more.
Be sure to take care of yourself, too.
I know. Honestly, this is already much better than what I was expecting, getting to keep him by my side.
Thank you, both of you, and bless the Reunion Movement!
...No need to thank me.
It's been two years, and you still feel uneasy when people show you their gratitude.
I thought you'd have gotten used to it long ago.
Well, I'm not the one they should be thanking.
Sure enough. They are united under Reunion's banner, but they should actually be thanking themselves.
Not everyone has the courage and ability to rebuild their lives from the rubble.
I hope they'll realize that.
And you might want to realize how obvious it is when your mind is drifting away.
I was just wondering... when is Hui-chieh going to come back again?
Don't tell me you're worried about her.
Every time she's here, she brings back news.
Catastrophe Messengers avoid this disputed area, and normal ones won't so much as pass through. The information network we've spent two years building up is still far from comprehensive.
I'm waiting... waiting for Hui-chieh to bring something more real.
What kind of news do you want to hear?
Anything... I want to know what's actually happening in Ursus right now.
You don't believe what we saw in the west.
What, you do?
The upper class gave a little handout out of kindness, and that was enough to quell the anger for the most part, and enough to make those beset by injustice forget to think and resist.
At the very least, we certainly did not receive any requests for help from the mining districts, whether from the west or even farther away.
Perhaps they just don't know of our existence, or they can't reach us.
Besides, we still have yet to build a truly stable stronghold here.
But the people here haven't lost hope in us because of it.
But they should, Nine.
Talulah looks back in the direction where the sun is hanging high in the sky.
She stares into its light, until her eyes start to hurt.
Even if our village is almost self-sufficient right now, an unforeseen Catastrophe could easily wipe everything out in one fell swoop.
The first couple months saw that happen again and again, and it took much trial-and-error for us to make it here.
But these peaceful days won't last long. I once believed that after our long journey, we'd have more companions leave us than remain. I never would've thought our group would grow larger.
And it's not because we ran into more oppressed people. It's because those who chose us haven't lost faith in us, even if they ought to have.
All we've done is show them a bit of kindness, and most of them chose to give unconditional trust in return.
Even after they've felt the pain of survival time and again, their barren lands can still yield these emotions more precious than gold?
I can't... After all that, I can't even imagine.
Which is why I started to think... What if it's just how everything is supposed to unfold? This is the truth that these humblest people believe.
They fall down, then get back up. They fail, and they start over.
What's your point?
We will fail again, Nine, but I think we don't have to fear this anymore.
Well, you're wrong. I've never been afraid of it.
But those who walked with us have felt that fear. After all, not everyone has such a grand outlook as you. Many of them just hope to see the next sunrise.
Nine, the new suppressant pipeline's finished. Should we put it to use right away?
Valiya.
Yeah?
If I were to tell you we're abandoning this place and heading to Deity Grypherburg, what would you do?
Hah! Of course I'm coming with you. Why?
What about the others?
Them too. We knew all along that Reunion wasn't going to stop here.
But I don't think all of us should go. Everyone's been doing different things here for a while. Those of us who can fight should go with you, but the rest can still stay here.
You're right.
Hmph...
Nine, it's just as he said... I think perhaps we're ready now—
We're ready to face the hideous truth hiding behind all that's bright and colorful. After they've long taken root and proliferated, they can't all be pulled at once...
In such a case, we can only keep pulling away. A second time, and a third time... for as long as we live, until someone finally succeeds.
Now that we know who we must bring together, the time may have come to break these shackles, and we should do it without hesitation.
Uniting all those who can unite, and breaking all chains that should never have chained us...
We need only a single opportunity.
Talulah. Nine.
It's urgent.
What is it, Red? It's not like you to look so nervous.
I ran into a group of refugees near the village. They said they're miners who escaped the Far North, and they're here cause they've heard tell of Reunion.
I checked them over. They're unarmed, and they're all Infected. I had them wait at the sentry post. We need you to decide what to do.
From the Far North? We haven't heard much from there. What do you mean, they 'escaped?'
Yeah, not much intel ever leaks out of there.
As for what happened... maybe it's better you hear it from them.
Thanks... Thanks for taking us in, kind lady, mister... Are you the Reunion Movement we heard about?
I never would've thought... what Rankin told us was true... My goodness...
Slow down. We are Reunion, and we will do what we can to help all our suffering comrades.
Could you please tell us what you've been through?
We...
There was a massacre in the Far North...
Th-The guns have stopped.
C-Can we go now? Not upwards, but down, before the bombs reach us—
Get up, Yuri, and the rest of you. We need to find Rankin and the place he was talking about.
You know the bravest ones of us have all left with Rankin, to take the outpost.
The bombing's stopped, and we don't know what's going on up there. The only way to find them is up.
Either we save them, or, as Rankin said, remember them. Crawl, pull yourselves on with your pickaxes, if you can't walk.
But Rankin gave us his mining lamp—
Do you remember what he said?
He... he didn't say anything...
That's right. He didn't say anything. This is no different from the supplies that they left behind.
Or we can choose to leave it here... as a reminder.
Yes, reminder. Leave the lamp in the mining cart, the one that we pushed all the way here.
If anyone ever comes through...
I remember that you can write, Yuri.
We've got some blank pages at the back of the logbook. We might as well not waste it.
Come here. I'll tell you what to write.
If anyone comes, at least they'll know what happened here.

lines.loc: A mine, Northern Tundra, UrsusWinter, 1101

lines.x: Sir, where... where are we going?
I don't think the factory's this way—
Shut up and keep walking.
I said walk!
(Whisper) The mines have stopped again.
(Whisper) So, nothing for the processing plants to work with.
(Whisper) Where would they take us, if they don't need hands upstairs?
They dragged even the sickest ones out of their beds. Half the mine is here...
I don't want to keep walking. I don't want to die!
(Whisper) Shh!
Go through the forest, to the lake. That's where you'll meet your end.
Don't even think...
About running away.
Heavy military boots step on the snowy forest floor, moving forward, urging forward.
The miners are being escorted by far more than just a few men.
Here. Stop.
Let's begin.
Everyone, on the ice.
No, sir, please—
The saber cuts her throat, spilling hot blood on the soldier's armor.
For the glory of Ursus.
Bozhe moy...
Proceed.
N-Next! Fill the gap! Hurry!
Why do we have to be treated like this?
Shut up, you uyobok—
Were the Tsar's promises all a lie? Are our lives worth less than that of a burdenbeast?
Tell me—
As the soldier is about to raise his hand again, the rumbling of the ground becomes perceptible.
It starts with a tiny crack, from the surface, from deep below, before snowballing quickly.
The ground trembles, and the center of the lake collapses. The result of geological changes accumulated over time swallows the Infected on the ice, and their would-be executioners.
Run!
The miner shakes off the stunned soldier and runs in the direction they came from.
Catastrophe...
It's a Catastrophe!!!
Soldier, miner. Killer, victim. The vast Lake Angara accepts all and delivers all.
Beneath the ice, an ancient nomadic city rises as the terrain changes, emerging through the sheet into the light of day.
The old-style buildings and the writhing faces of the dead look the same as they did when the city sank.

lines.loc: Reconstruction District, Far North, Ursus

lines.x: 'Northern Tundra'... 'Catastrophe'... 'Lake'...
All those words crammed into this tiny space, and I only recognize three.
You'll learn them in time.
The lessons here are too basic for me. I'm planning to take over the work at the mines, which has been left untended for a while, and maybe learn a craft other than shoe repair.
Once the curfew is lifted, I want to go to Deity Grypherburg.
I don't want to go to Grypherburg. I just want to learn to read. Three words a day makes twenty-one a week. In one month...
I'm glad to see your eagerness.
Do you need help?
These words still confuse me, gospozha.
You're talking about these 'nouns', right? That's the lesson for this week—we're learning about nouns.
Nouns, yes! Nouns. I think I'm starting to be able to tell them apart, but I can't quite get their meanings.
What's this... uh... 'Shimok'?
Ah...
It's 'Simonch'. It's a name, like the name 'Angara', the lake, or your name, 'Vova'.
It's the name of a nomadic city.
The youth bids his classmate farewell and goes into the mines, ready to get to work.
Mine tunnels left untended gradually wear down and eventually collapse. He will not allow it to happen, as long as he is still here.
The young man raises his mining lamp and begins his rounds, examining all the rail, until he finds a mining cart at one of the forks that does not belong to this place.
This is...
A mining lamp?
And a letter—
This was once the place of a struggle?
Damn it. The people who proposed connecting the mining districts were here! They wanted to occupy that garrisoned outpost!
I knew the story was true...
Phew...
Whoever's lamp this was, it's in good condition. Maybe a little dusty, but it still works...
Maybe I should take it to Grypherburg—
And show everyone...
This lamp without a name, and those people without names.
'All units, this is Dispatch.'
'All units, cease fire immediately. We have an urgent request from Deity Grypherburg.'
'Repeat, all units, cease fire immediately.'
'Please acknowledge.'
(Exhales)
Scope of the ceasefire?
All active units.
(Nods)
'Acknowledged.'
Inform Lieutenant Yelchin and ensure that all units in the 2nd Division observe the ceasefire.
'All units remain on standby. Maintain battle stations.'
'Until the threat is completely eliminated, we shall continue to serve our duty to protect Deity Grypherburg and the people.'
...Summon the quartermaster.
Sir, we do not have a quartermaster for this operation.
You expected this to be only a quick response, and that it would not take much time to eliminate the rebellious Infected.
Respond to Dispatch as usual.
And what is your name, soldier?
Private Vyacheslav Kinov, sir.
Very well. Inform the Lieutenant, Private Kinov. Your services are not needed here for now.
...You had me leave my post to come to you for this little thing?
You're out of your mind.
Oh, look at you, Patya, all high and mighty in that Shieldguard armor...
Does that pretty helmet of yours block your sight?
The maggots in the rations, bolts that snap like twigs, even duds!
Is this what they send us out to die with?!
If there are maggots in your rations, you eat them with the rations. If the weapons are hard to use, use your bare hands.
We're soldiers. We fight and win. There are only two ends: Keep fighting, or die.
You're the one who's out of his mind!
If you find that someone hasn't been doing his job, you report to your superior, not complain to me here.
I'm not the quartermaster—
I thought the Defender Regiments were supposed to be on the top deck.
...Sorry, sir. I was away from my post.
I will report this to the commissar myself.
(Salutes)
S-Sir!
What did you find out, soldier?
Tell me the reason for your anger.
...The quality of our supplies and equipment on this operation is exceedingly poor.
The rations are insufficient, the cans damaged and filled with maggots...
Many of the bolts were not made of steel, but crude iron.
And the reserve Originium bombs are so weak that they're little more than glorified duds.
How are we supposed to proceed the operation with these?
Why did you enlist, soldier?
To...
Speak up.
To—to prove myself! I want people to know my name—
Like they know yours!
They'll know your name when the Army sends your tags back to your mother, and she etches them on a white birch tree.
No, not like that...
There is little room for the individual to choose, when the collective is at war.
But...
Keep this hope in your heart, boy, and your 'discovery' too.
For the survival of Ursus and her people, there is only one option...
Set this land on fire.

lines.st: *huff*... *huff*...
It hurts... the rocks are piercing the flesh... I can almost hear them growing. Why am I still alive?
I'm exhausted. I can't feel my limbs... Run! Run to the east!
Death awaits in the south. Death awaits in the north. In the west... the mines there are a dead end, just like those in the Far North.
I can run from the Far North... but how do I run from pain?

lines.loc: Far North Border, Ursus

lines.x: Urgh...
I can't... run... anymore...
This is it...
Is someone there?
I'll take a look—
No, please let me...
I can sense that it's only a poor soul who has suffered much.
Exhausted, dehydrated, starved...
You're Infected too.
*cough* *cough*!
He's one of us, comrades!
Please, save him, just as you saved all of us—
Perform your miracle!
It's not a miracle...
Oripathy is not the main reason for his pain. I can only stop his illness from tormenting him, but I cannot give him food, clean water, or a safe place to sleep.
I can't perform miracles. I'm not all-powerful. I simply... believe.
You're always so humble. We understand.
...Can you hear me, sir?
I'll hold your hand...
'Believe. Believe in our life.'
'Believe in our body and mind, believe every beat of our heart, believe every inhale and exhale.'
'Believe that we are the source of all strength.'
No longer will you live in fear of death.
The pain brought about by the rapid growth of Originium in his body begins to fade.
He can still hear the voices, but he is no longer writhing in pain.
Wh-Who are you?
You saved me...
*cough* *cough*—
What can I do to repay you?
You can call me Hierda, like everyone else here.
A miracle—
Hierda has once again saved our comrade!
She saved all of us! With her motherly caress, she takes away our pain!
Hierda!
Hierda.
Hierda!
Hierda... Hierda.
Yes, that is my name.
What do you look like? I can't see you clearly, or any of the others...
Your head is wounded. Did you fall and hurt yourself?
Maybe a mild brain hemorrhage is affecting your vision. Don't worry, we can help you walk...
Where are you going?
I don't know...
Then come with us, for now.
You're taking us to a place without pain, right?
Yes, as long as you're willing to come with me...
As long as we believe.
